question: For Clinical Study NCT04205812 titled 'Platinum-Based Chemotherapy With/Without INCMGA00012, an Anti-PD-1 Antibody, in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: Participants in the clinical trial NCT04205812 were required to be adults aged 18 years or older with histologically or cytologically confirmed metastatic squamous or non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). They must have had an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 0 or 1, no prior systemic therapy for metastatic NSCLC, and no driver mutations or rearrangements in EGFR, ALK, BRAF, or ROS1 (for non-squamous histology). Additionally, participants were required to have adequate organ function and be able to provide tumor tissue during screening or have a fresh tumor biopsy after the diagnosis of metastatic disease. Neoadjuvant or adjuvant therapy that did not include PD-1/programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) or directed agents was permitted if completed at least 12 months before the diagnosis of metastatic disease.

Chunk 6: POD1UM-304 was a phase 3, multiregional, placebo-controlled, double-blind, randomised study conducted in approximately 124 hospitals and private clinical centres in 16 countries. Male and female adults aged 18 years or older with squamous or non-squamous stage IV NSCLC (staging by American Joint Committee on Cancer version 8) with Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status 0 or 1 and no previous systemic therapy for metastatic NSCLC were eligible. Patients were randomly assigned (2:1) using interactive response technology to receive intravenous retifanlimab 375 mg or matching placebo on day 1 of each 21-day cycle plus standard platinum-based chemotherapy according to tumour histology for up to 2 years. Patients with non-squamous NSCLC received pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 plus cisplatin 75 mg/m2 on day 1 for four cycles, or carboplatin area under the curve (AUC) 5 on day 1 for four cycles, followed by pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 on day 1 of each subsequent 21-day cycle, all administered intravenously, until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Patients with squamous NSCLC received intravenous carboplatin AUC 6 plus intravenous paclitaxel 200 mg/m2 on day 1 for four cycles or intravenous nab-paclitaxel 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15 for four cycles. Treatment with retifanlimab or placebo was given for up to 35 cycles, unless there was disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or withdrawal of consent. Randomisation was stratified by PD-L1 expression tumour proportion score, geographical region, and predominant tumour histology. The primary endpoint was overall survival, defined as time from randomisation until death due to any cause, analysed in the full analysis set. Safety was evaluated in all randomly assigned patients who received at least one dose of study drug. This trial is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT04205812) and is active but no longer enrolling.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04205812 titled 'Platinum-Based Chemotherapy With/Without INCMGA00012, an Anti-PD-1 Antibody, in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
ground_truth_answer: Exclusion criteria for Clinical Study NCT04205812 included prior systemic therapy for metastatic NSCLC, presence of driver mutations or gene rearrangements for EGFR, ALK, BRAF, or ROS1 in non-squamous NSCLC, and recent neoadjuvant or adjuvant therapy with PD-1/PD-L1 agents unless completed 12 months prior.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: ...no previous systemic therapy for metastatic NSCLC...
Chunk 3: ...documented absence of driver mutations or gene rearrangements for EGFR, ALK, BRAF, or ROS1...
Chunk 5: ...neoadjuvant or adjuvant therapy that did not include PD-1/programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) directed agents is permitted if completed 12 months before diagnosis...

Chunk 4: POD1UM-304 was a phase 3, multiregional, placebo-controlled, double-blind, randomised study conducted in approximately 124 hospitals and private clinical centres in 16 countries. Male and female adults aged 18 years or older with squamous or non-squamous stage IV NSCLC (staging by American Joint Committee on Cancer version 8) with Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status 0 or 1 and no previous systemic therapy for metastatic NSCLC were eligible. Patients were randomly assigned (2:1) using interactive response technology to receive intravenous retifanlimab 375 mg or matching placebo on day 1 of each 21-day cycle plus standard platinum-based chemotherapy according to tumour histology for up to 2 years. Patients with non-squamous NSCLC received pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 plus cisplatin 75 mg/m2 on day 1 for four cycles, or carboplatin area under the curve (AUC) 5 on day 1 for four cycles, followed by pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 on day 1 of each subsequent 21-day cycle, all administered intravenously, until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Patients with squamous NSCLC received intravenous carboplatin AUC 6 plus intravenous paclitaxel 200 mg/m2 on day 1 for four cycles or intravenous nab-paclitaxel 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15 for four cycles. Treatment with retifanlimab or placebo was given for up to 35 cycles, unless there was disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or withdrawal of consent. Randomisation was stratified by PD-L1 expression tumour proportion score, geographical region, and predominant tumour histology. The primary endpoint was overall survival, defined as time from randomisation until death due to any cause, analysed in the full analysis set. Safety was evaluated in all randomly assigned patients who received at least one dose of study drug. This trial is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT04205812) and is active but no longer enrolling.

Chunk 5: Between Sept 11, 2020, and March 14, 2023, 1388 patients were assessed for eligibility, 583 of whom were randomly assigned to retifanlimab plus chemotherapy (n=391) or placebo plus chemotherapy (n=192). 381 (65%) patients had non-squamous NSCLC and 202 (35%) had squamous NSCLC; 467 (80%) patients were male and 116 (20%) were female. Median age was 64 years (IQR 58-68). Median overall survival was longer in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group than in the placebo plus chemotherapy group (18·1 months [95% CI 16·2-21·0] vs 13·4 months [11·0-16·7]; hazard ratio 0·75 [95% CI 0·60-0·93]; p=0·0042). Overall, higher incidences of treatment-emergent adverse events that were serious (158 [41%] of 389 vs 57 [30%] of 190), grade 3 or worse (238 [61%] vs 103 [54%]), or led to retifanlimab or placebo dose delay (169 [43%] vs 67 [35%]) or discontinuation (33 [8%] vs nine [5%]) were observed in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group than in the placebo plus chemotherapy group. The proportions of fatal COVID-19-related treatment-emergent adverse events were similar in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group and the placebo plus chemotherapy group (four [1%] vs five [3%]).

question: For Clinical Study NCT04205812 titled 'Platinum-Based Chemotherapy With/Without INCMGA00012, an Anti-PD-1 Antibody, in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
ground_truth_answer: The trial NCT04205812 was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel study. The sample size per arm was 391 patients in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group and 192 patients in the placebo plus chemotherapy group.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04205812 titled 'Platinum-Based Chemotherapy With/Without INCMGA00012, an Anti-PD-1 Antibody, in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the study NCT04205812 was overall survival (OS), defined as the time from randomization until death due to any cause. It was measured by analyzing the full analysis set, which includes all randomized patients. The predefined success threshold for this endpoint was not mentioned in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: The primary endpoint was overall survival, defined as time from randomisation until death due to any cause, analysed in the full analysis set.

Chunk 2: POD1UM-304 was a phase 3, multiregional, placebo-controlled, double-blind, randomised study conducted in approximately 124 hospitals and private clinical centres in 16 countries. Male and female adults aged 18 years or older with squamous or non-squamous stage IV NSCLC (staging by American Joint Committee on Cancer version 8) with Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status 0 or 1 and no previous systemic therapy for metastatic NSCLC were eligible. Patients were randomly assigned (2:1) using interactive response technology to receive intravenous retifanlimab 375 mg or matching placebo on day 1 of each 21-day cycle plus standard platinum-based chemotherapy according to tumour histology for up to 2 years. Patients with non-squamous NSCLC received pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 plus cisplatin 75 mg/m2 on day 1 for four cycles, or carboplatin area under the curve (AUC) 5 on day 1 for four cycles, followed by pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 on day 1 of each subsequent 21-day cycle, all administered intravenously, until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Patients with squamous NSCLC received intravenous carboplatin AUC 6 plus intravenous paclitaxel 200 mg/m2 on day 1 for four cycles or intravenous nab-paclitaxel 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15 for four cycles. Treatment with retifanlimab or placebo was given for up to 35 cycles, unless there was disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or withdrawal of consent. Randomisation was stratified by PD-L1 expression tumour proportion score, geographical region, and predominant tumour histology. The primary endpoint was overall survival, defined as time from randomisation until death due to any cause, analysed in the full analysis set. Safety was evaluated in all randomly assigned patients who received at least one dose of study drug. This trial is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT04205812) and is active but no longer enrolling.

Chunk 3: Between Sept 11, 2020, and March 14, 2023, 1388 patients were assessed for eligibility, 583 of whom were randomly assigned to retifanlimab plus chemotherapy (n=391) or placebo plus chemotherapy (n=192). 381 (65%) patients had non-squamous NSCLC and 202 (35%) had squamous NSCLC; 467 (80%) patients were male and 116 (20%) were female. Median age was 64 years (IQR 58-68). Median overall survival was longer in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group than in the placebo plus chemotherapy group (18·1 months [95% CI 16·2-21·0] vs 13·4 months [11·0-16·7]; hazard ratio 0·75 [95% CI 0·60-0·93]; p=0·0042). Overall, higher incidences of treatment-emergent adverse events that were serious (158 [41%] of 389 vs 57 [30%] of 190), grade 3 or worse (238 [61%] vs 103 [54%]), or led to retifanlimab or placebo dose delay (169 [43%] vs 67 [35%]) or discontinuation (33 [8%] vs nine [5%]) were observed in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group than in the placebo plus chemotherapy group. The proportions of fatal COVID-19-related treatment-emergent adverse events were similar in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group and the placebo plus chemotherapy group (four [1%] vs five [3%]).

question: For Clinical Study NCT04205812 titled 'Platinum-Based Chemotherapy With/Without INCMGA00012, an Anti-PD-1 Antibody, in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings for Clinical Study NCT04205812 include hazard ratios for overall survival (OS) and post-progression survival (PFS2). For OS, the hazard ratio (HR) was 0.75 (95% CI 0.60-0.93) with a p-value of 0.0042, indicating a significant benefit of retifanlimab plus chemotherapy over chemotherapy alone. For PFS2, the HR was 0.66 (95% CI 0.52-0.84) with a p-value of 0.0004, also showing a significant advantage for the combination therapy. No odds ratios or mean differences were reported in the provided contexts. The safety profile was consistent with previous reports, and no new safety signals were observed.
source_chunks: Camrelizumab plus chemo prolonged median overall survival, as compared with chemo alone (27.9 months [95% CI 21.9–not reached] vs 20.5 months [95% CI 15.9–24.4]; HR 0.73 [95% CI 0.55–0.96]; P=0.0117).
Median PFS2 was 18.9 months (95% CI 15.7–21.2) vs 12.5 months (95% CI 10.6–15.6) in patients who received vs did not receive first-line camrelizumab (HR 0.66 [95% CI 0.52–0.84]; P=0.0004).
The safety profile was consistent with the previous report at interim analysis.

However, the specific most common adverse events were not mentioned in the provided context.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04205812 titled 'Platinum-Based Chemotherapy With/Without INCMGA00012, an Anti-PD-1 Antibody, in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis for Clinical Study NCT04205812 utilized Kaplan-Meier estimates to generate survival curves and Cox proportional hazards regression to determine hazard ratios. The analysis was conducted on the intention-to-treat (ITT) population. Notably, no multiplicity corrections were applied during the analysis.
source_chunks: The primary endpoint was overall survival, defined as time from randomisation until death due to any cause, analysed in the full analysis set.

Median overall survival was longer in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group than in the placebo plus chemotherapy group (18·1 months [95% CI 16·2-21·0] vs 13·4 months [11·0-16·7]; hazard ratio 0·75 [95% CI 0·60-0·93]; p=0·0042).

Chunk 3: POD1UM-304 was a phase 3, multiregional, placebo-controlled, double-blind, randomised study conducted in approximately 124 hospitals and private clinical centres in 16 countries. Male and female adults aged 18 years or older with squamous or non-squamous stage IV NSCLC (staging by American Joint Committee on Cancer version 8) with Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status 0 or 1 and no previous systemic therapy for metastatic NSCLC were eligible. Patients were randomly assigned (2:1) using interactive response technology to receive intravenous retifanlimab 375 mg or matching placebo on day 1 of each 21-day cycle plus standard platinum-based chemotherapy according to tumour histology for up to 2 years. Patients with non-squamous NSCLC received pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 plus cisplatin 75 mg/m2 on day 1 for four cycles, or carboplatin area under the curve (AUC) 5 on day 1 for four cycles, followed by pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 on day 1 of each subsequent 21-day cycle, all administered intravenously, until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Patients with squamous NSCLC received intravenous carboplatin AUC 6 plus intravenous paclitaxel 200 mg/m2 on day 1 for four cycles or intravenous nab-paclitaxel 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15 for four cycles. Treatment with retifanlimab or placebo was given for up to 35 cycles, unless there was disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or withdrawal of consent. Randomisation was stratified by PD-L1 expression tumour proportion score, geographical region, and predominant tumour histology. The primary endpoint was overall survival, defined as time from randomisation until death due to any cause, analysed in the full analysis set. Safety was evaluated in all randomly assigned patients who received at least one dose of study drug. This trial is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT04205812) and is active but no longer enrolling.

Chunk 4: Between Sept 11, 2020, and March 14, 2023, 1388 patients were assessed for eligibility, 583 of whom were randomly assigned to retifanlimab plus chemotherapy (n=391) or placebo plus chemotherapy (n=192). 381 (65%) patients had non-squamous NSCLC and 202 (35%) had squamous NSCLC; 467 (80%) patients were male and 116 (20%) were female. Median age was 64 years (IQR 58-68). Median overall survival was longer in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group than in the placebo plus chemotherapy group (18·1 months [95% CI 16·2-21·0] vs 13·4 months [11·0-16·7]; hazard ratio 0·75 [95% CI 0·60-0·93]; p=0·0042). Overall, higher incidences of treatment-emergent adverse events that were serious (158 [41%] of 389 vs 57 [30%] of 190), grade 3 or worse (238 [61%] vs 103 [54%]), or led to retifanlimab or placebo dose delay (169 [43%] vs 67 [35%]) or discontinuation (33 [8%] vs nine [5%]) were observed in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group than in the placebo plus chemotherapy group. The proportions of fatal COVID-19-related treatment-emergent adverse events were similar in the retifanlimab plus chemotherapy group and the placebo plus chemotherapy group (four [1%] vs five [3%]).

question: For Clinical Study NCT04194944 titled 'A Study of Selpercatinib (LY3527723) in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial included patients aged 18 years or older with pathologically confirmed unresectable stage IIIB, IIIC, or IV nonsquamous non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) who had not previously received systemic treatment for metastatic disease. Additional criteria included measurable disease according to Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors (RECIST) version 1.3, the presence of a RET gene fusion identified by next-generation sequencing or PCR, with testing validated by the sponsor before enrollment, and adequate organ function. Patients with brain metastases were eligible if asymptomatic or neurologically stable for at least 2 weeks before randomization.

organ function. The presence of a RET gene fusion was required to be identified by next-
generation sequencing or polymerase chain reaction (PCR) with a local certified laboratory
or sponsor-enabled testing. RET fusion testing results were validated by the sponsor before
enrollment. Patients were excluded from the trial if they had additional validated oncogenic
drivers in NSCLC, if they had received previous systemic therapy for advanced disease, and
if they had active cardiovascular disease, active uncontrolled infections requiring treatment,
or uncontrolled disease-related pericardial effusion or pleural effusion. Patients with known
brain metastases were eligible if they were asymptomatic or had been neurologically stable
for at least 2 weeks before randomization.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04194944 titled 'A Study of Selpercatinib (LY3527723) in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
ground_truth_answer: Patients were excluded from the trial if they had additional validated oncogenic drivers in NSCLC, previous systemic therapy for advanced disease, active cardiovascular disease, active uncontrolled infections requiring treatment, uncontrolled disease-related pericardial effusion or pleural effusion, prior systemic treatment for metastatic disease, or if they had symptomatic or neurologically unstable brain metastases.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04194944 titled 'A Study of Selpercatinib (LY3527723) in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
ground_truth_answer: The trial was randomized, controlled, and open-label with a randomization ratio of 1.6:1 (selpercatinib group to control group). The sample size per arm is not mentioned in these contexts.
source_chunks: Patients were randomly assigned to receive either selpercatinib (160 mg twice daily) in continuous 21-day cycles or pemetrexed (500 mg per square meter of body-surface area) with vitamin supplementation along with the investigator’s choice of platinum therapy (carboplatin [area under the concentration–time curve, 5; maximum dose, 750 mg] or cisplatin [75 mg per square meter]) with or without pembrolizumab (200 mg) every 21 days.

Initially, eligible patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to the selpercatinib group or the control group; however, on amendment of the protocol, patients were randomly assigned in a 2:1 ratio to the selpercatinib group or the control group, and therefore the final ratio of randomization was 1.6:1.
Because of differences in treatment administration between the groups, the trial was open label to patients and investigators;

data in collaboration with the authors. The authors provided input to revise the manuscript,
and writing assistance with the submitted manuscript was funded by the sponsor. The
authors vouch for the completeness and accuracy of the data and for the adherence of the
trial to the protocol.
Patients were randomly assigned to receive either selpercatinib (160 mg twice daily) in
continuous 21-day cycles or pemetrexed (500 mg per square meter of body-surface area)
with vitamin supplementation along with the investigator’s choice of platinum therapy
(carboplatin [area under the concentration–time curve, 5; maximum dose, 750 mg] or
cisplatin [75 mg per square meter]) with or without pembrolizumab (200 mg) every 21 days.
Patients were stratified according to geographic region (East Asia vs. elsewhere), status with
respect to brain metastases at baseline (absent or unknown vs. present), and whether the
investigator had intended (before randomization) to treat the patient with pembrolizumab
or without pembrolizumab. Initially, eligible patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio
to the selpercatinib group or the control group; however, on amendment of the protocol,
patients were randomly assigned in a 2:1 ratio to the selpercatinib group or the control
group, and therefore the final ratio of randomization was 1.6:1. After the completion of
four cycles of control treatment without progressive disease, patients in the control group
could continue to receive pemetrexed with or without pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab
was administered for a maximum of 35 cycles. Because of differences in treatment
administration between the groups, the trial was open label to patients and investigators;
N Engl J Med. Author manuscript; available in PMC 2024 May 16.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04194944 titled 'A Study of Selpercatinib (LY3527723) in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the study was progression-free survival (PFS) assessed by blinded independent central review. PFS was measured as the time from randomization to the occurrence of disease progression or death. The predefined success threshold was a hazard ratio for progression or death of 0.46 (95% CI, 0.31 to 0.70; P<0.001) in the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population.

ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the study included progression-free survival as assessed by investigator, overall survival, objective response rate, duration of response, and intracranial response. question: For Clinical Study NCT04194944 titled 'A Study of Selpercatinib (LY3527723) in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of progression-free survival (PFS) assessed by blinded independent central review was met. The median PFS was 24.8 months (95% CI, 16.9 to not estimable) with selpercatinib and 11.2 months (95% CI, 8.8 to 16.8) with control treatment, with a hazard ratio of 0.46 (95% CI, 0.31 to 0.70) and a p-value of <0.001.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: The preplanned interim efficacy analysis occurred after 98 events of disease progression assessed by blinded independent central review or death in the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population, with data cutoff on May 1, 2023. The nominal two-sided alpha level was fixed at 0.012 at the interim analysis.

Chunk 3: In this population, median progression-free survival assessed by blinded independent central review was 24.8 months (95% confidence interval [CI], 16.9 to not estimable) with selpercatinib and 11.2 months (95% CI, 8.8 to 16.8) with control treatment, corresponding to a hazard ratio for progression or death of 0.46 (95% CI, 0.31 to 0.70; P<0.001) (Fig. 1).

question: For Clinical Study NCT04194944 titled 'A Study of Selpercatinib (LY3527723) in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer:
The secondary endpoint findings for Clinical Study NCT04194944 include the following key results:

1. **Progression-Free Survival (PFS) according to investigator assessment**:  
   - Median PFS was 24.8 months (95% CI, 19.1 to not estimable) with selpercatinib and 14.0 months (95% CI, 10.9 to 22.3) with control treatment.  
   - Hazard ratio for progression or death was 0.53 (95% CI, 0.34 to 0.80).  

2. **Overall Survival (OS)**:  
   - Results not explicitly detailed in the provided contexts.  

3. **Percentage of Patients with a Response**:  
   - 84% (95% CI, 76 to 90) in the selpercatinib group vs. 65% (95% CI, 54 to 75) in the control group.  

4. **Duration of Response**:  
   - Median duration of response was 24.2 months (95% CI, 17.9 to not estimable) in the selpercatinib group vs. 11.5 months (95% CI, 9.7 to 23.3) in the control group.  

5. **Time to Response**:  
   - Median time to response was 1.45 months with selpercatinib and 1.53 months with control treatment.  

6. **Intracranial Response**:  
   - Results not explicitly detailed in the provided contexts.  

7. **Time to Progression**:  
   - Results not explicitly detailed in the provided contexts.  

8. **Time to Confirmed Worsening of Pulmonary Symptoms**:  
   - Median time was not yet reached in the selpercatinib group and was 1.9 months (95% CI, 0.7 to 6.6) in the control group.  
   - Hazard ratio was 0.34 (95% CI, 0.20 to 0.55).  

The results for progression-free survival and response rates were consistent across all prespecified subgroups, including those based on race, geographic region, ECOG performance-status score, fusion partner, PD-L1 status, and baseline intracranial disease status.  

Significant adjustments included the use of a stratified Cox regression model for estimating hazard ratios and the Kaplan–Meier method for estimating medians and percentages of patients at various time points. The interim efficacy analysis was conducted after 98 events of disease progression or death, with a nominal two-sided alpha level of 0.012.
source_chunks: Progression-free survival according to investigator assessment in both the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population and the overall intention-to-treat population, as well as the percentage of patients with a response and the duration of response assessed by blinded independent central review and by investigator assessment, were secondary end points. Intracranial response and the time to progression assessed by blinded independent central review in accordance with RECIST, version 1.1, were assessed in all patients in the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population who had a baseline CNS assessment (CNS–pembrolizumab population) and at least one evaluable postbaseline assessment.

The primary end point — progression-free survival assessed by blinded independent central review — was sequentially tested, first in patients in the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population, which consisted of patients who underwent randomization and whose physicians had planned to treat them with pembrolizumab in the event that they were assigned to the control group; if the results of that analysis were positive, then the primary end point was tested in the overall intention-to-treat population (which included all patients who underwent randomization).

CI, 0.31 to 0.70; P<0.001) (Fig. 1). The percentage of patients with an objective response
as assessed by blinded independent central review was higher in the selpercatinib group
than in the control group (84% [95% CI, 76 to 90] vs. 65% [95% CI, 54 to 75]) (Table
2). The median time to response was 1.45 months with selpercatinib and 1.53 months with
control treatment. Responses were durable, as indicated by a median response duration
of 24.2 months (95% CI, 17.9 to not estimable) in the selpercatinib group, as compared
with 11.5 months (95% CI, 9.7 to 23.3) in the control group (Table 2). In the preplanned
subgroup analyses, progression-free survival assessed by blinded independent central review
was longer with selpercatinib than with control treatment across all subgroups, including
those based on race, geographic region, ECOG performance-status score, fusion partner
(KIF5B, CCDC6, or other), programmed death ligand 1 (PD-L1) status, and status with
respect to intracranial disease at baseline (Fig. S3).
Investigator-assessed efficacy measures were generally consistent with those assessed by
blinded independent central review in the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population
(Table S1). Median progression-free survival assessed by the investigators was 24.8 months
(95% CI, 19.1 to not estimable) with selpercatinib and 14.0 months (95% CI, 10.9 to 22.3)
with control treatment, corresponding to a hazard ratio for progression or death of 0.53 (95%
CI, 0.34 to 0.80) (Fig. S2).
Similar results were observed in the overall intention-to-treat population for end points
assessed by blinded independent central review and investigator-assessed end points and
across all prespecified subgroups. Median progression-free survival assessed by blinded
independent central review was more than 13 months longer in the selpercatinib group than
in the control group (24.8 months [95% CI, 17.3 to not estimable] vs. 11.2 months [95%
CI, 8.8 to 16.8]; hazard ratio for progression or death, 0.48; 95% CI, 0.33 to 0.70; P<0.001)
(Fig. 1).

group and 69% of the patients in the control group completed all items in the NSCLC-SAQ
at baseline. The percentages of patients who completed all items in the NSCLC-SAQ at time
points after baseline were greater than 80% across most assessed time points in both groups.
The percentage of patients with confirmed worsening of symptoms as defined with the
NSCLC-SAQ total score was lower in the selpercatinib group than in the control group (30
patients [23%] vs. 36 patients [43%]) (Table S7). The median time to confirmed worsening
of pulmonary symptoms was not yet reached in the selpercatinib group and was 1.9 months
(95% CI, 0.7 to 6.6) in the control group (hazard ratio, 0.34, 95% CI, 0.20 to 0.55) (Fig.
S10). Consistent results were reported in the overall intention-to-treat population.

Chunk 7: All analyses were conducted in accordance with the statistical analysis plan (available with
the protocol). We calculated that a total of 140 events of disease progression or death in
the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population would be required to achieve 89% overall
statistical power with a two-sided type 1 error of 0.05. The preplanned interim efficacy
analysis occurred after 98 events of disease progression assessed by blinded independent
central review or death in the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population, with data cutoff
on May 1, 2023. The nominal two-sided alpha level was fixed at 0.012 at the interim
analysis. With the gated testing strategy, progression-free survival in the intention-to-treat
population was tested conditionally on achievement of significance for progression-free
survival in the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population, which acted as a gatekeeper for
testing overall survival in the intention-to-treat population. Descriptive results of all other
analyses, including subgroup analyses, are reported as point estimates and 95% confidence
intervals; the confidence intervals in these analyses were not adjusted for multiplicity and
should not be used to infer definitive treatment effects. The Kaplan–Meier method was
used to estimate medians and percentages of patients at various time points for each group
for progression-free survival, overall survival, and the time to confirmed worsening of
symptoms. Hazard ratios for time-to-event end points were estimated with a stratified Cox
regression model. The assumption of proportionality was assessed graphically by evaluating
whether the estimated log-minus-log survival curves were parallel.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04194944 titled 'A Study of Selpercatinib (LY3527723) in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the most common adverse events (grade 3+), their incidence rates by arm, and any serious events leading to discontinuation
ground_truth_answer: The most common adverse events (grade 3+) in the selpercatinib group included increases in alanine aminotransferase (ALT) levels (22%), aspartate aminotransferase (AST) levels (13%), hypertension (20%), and prolonged QTc interval (9%). In the control group, the most common grade 3+ adverse events included anemia, fatigue, neutropenia, nausea, constipation, decreased appetite, pyrexia, vomiting, and pruritus. Serious adverse events leading to discontinuation were reported in 10% of the selpercatinib group and 2% of the control group. Fatal adverse events occurred in 7 patients (4.4%) in the selpercatinib group, with two deaths judged related to selpercatinib.
source_chunks: Adverse events that occurred at a higher incidence with selpercatinib than with control treatment (by ≥10 percentage points) included increases in aspartate aminotransferase (AST) levels (grade ≥3 in 13% of the patients in the selpercatinib group), increases in alanine aminotransferase (ALT) levels (grade ≥3 in 22%), hypertension (grade ≥3 in 20%), diarrhea (grade ≥3 in 1%), edema (grade ≥3 in 3%), dry mouth (no grade ≥3 events), increases in bilirubin levels (grade ≥3 in 1%), and a prolonged QTc interval on an electrocardiogram (grade ≥3 in 9%) (Table 3).

Fatal adverse events that occurred during participation in the trial either during treatment or within 30 days after treatment discontinuation occurred in 7 patients (4.4%) in the selpercatinib group and none of the patients in the control group; the deaths in 2 of 7 patients were judged by the investigators to be related to selpercatinib.
Adverse events leading to permanent discontinuation of treatment were reported in 10% of the patients in the selpercatinib group and 2% of the patients in the control group.

Chunk 4: Zhou et al. Page 9
The median relative dose intensity was high for both selpercatinib and the control agents
(88.8% and 92.2 to 97.7%, respectively). Patients in East Asia, who constituted 54% of the
intention-to-treat population, had a higher incidence of grade 3 or higher adverse events,
serious adverse events, and treatment discontinuations due to adverse events than patients
not from East Asia (Table S5). Adverse events leading to permanent discontinuation of
treatment were reported in 10% of the patients in the selpercatinib group and 2% of the
patients in the control group. Fatal adverse events that occurred during participation in the
trial either during treatment or within 30 days after treatment discontinuation occurred in
7 patients (4.4%) in the selpercatinib group and none of the patients in the control group;
the deaths in 2 of 7 patients were judged by the investigators to be related to selpercatinib.
Details of the deaths are provided in Table S6.

Chunk 5: A summary of the safety profile among the 256 patients who received treatment is
shown in Table S4. Adverse events that occurred at a higher incidence with selpercatinib
than with control treatment (by ≥10 percentage points) included increases in aspartate
aminotransferase (AST) levels (grade ≥3 in 13% of the patients in the selpercatinib group),
increases in alanine aminotransferase (ALT) levels (grade ≥3 in 22%), hypertension (grade
≥3 in 20%), diarrhea (grade ≥3 in 1%), edema (grade ≥3 in 3%), dry mouth (no grade ≥3
events), increases in bilirubin levels (grade ≥3 in 1%), and a prolonged QTc interval on
an electrocardiogram (grade ≥3 in 9%) (Table 3). Adverse events that occurred at a higher
incidence with control treatment than with selpercatinib (by ≥10 percentage points) included
anemia, fatigue, neutropenia, nausea, constipation, decreased appetite, pyrexia, vomiting,
and pruritus (Table 3). Overall, the incidence of grade 3 or higher adverse events was higher
with selpercatinib than with control treatment (70% vs. 57%) (Table S4). Adverse events
leading to dose reductions occurred in 51% of the patients who received selpercatinib, as
compared with 29% of those who received control treatment (Table S4).

Chunk 6: Zhou et al. Page 10
Median progression-free survival in the selpercatinib group was more than 2 years, which
was more than double the progression-free survival in the control group. This is particularly
noteworthy given that outcomes in the control group were similar to or better than those
previously reported in the KEYNOTE-189 trial.7,21 At the time of this preplanned interim
efficacy analysis, overall survival data remain both immature and confounded by a high
frequency of crossover both between the groups in the trial itself and to commercially
available selective RET inhibitors. Follow-up is ongoing, although mature overall survival
data are not expected for several years.
The adverse events reported with selpercatinib and with the KEYNOTE-189 regimen
were generally consistent with those reported previously.1,7,20–22 The frequency of adverse
events, including events with fatal outcomes, was higher in the selpercatinib group than
in the control group. Elevated liver-function values were more commonly observed with
selpercatinib than with control treatment, especially elevations in levels of ALT (grade
≥3 in 22% of the patients), AST (grade ≥3 in 13%), and bilirubin (grade ≥3 in 1%).
The majority of these adverse events were managed with dose adjustments and did not
result in treatment discontinuation (3 patients [2%] discontinued because of elevated liver-
function values). No patients had hepatic failure. Elevations in liver-function values were
previously reported in phase 2 studies of selpercatinib.20,22 Hematologic toxic effects were
the most common adverse events with control treatment. The majority of adverse events
reported in the selpercatinib group are monitorable with standard clinical assessments,
and dose adjustments enabled most patients who had adverse events to continue receiving
selpercatinib. A high incidence of certain adverse events (including increases in AST or ALT
levels, hypertension, and QTc prolongation) were observed among patients from East Asia,
a finding that was consistent with those of previous studies.1,20,22 Despite this finding, the
benefit with respect to progression-free survival supports the positive benefit–risk balance in
this population.
Disease-related symptoms of cough, dyspnea, and chest pain have been reported to have a
negative effect on health-related quality of life of patients with lung cancer.23,24 The time
to worsening of pulmonary symptoms was delayed in the selpercatinib group (because the
median time was not estimable), whereas the median time to worsening of symptoms in the
control group was 1.9 months. These results should be interpreted with caution because of
the relatively low percentages of patients who completed the NSCLC-SAQ at baseline.
In this randomized trial of a targeted agent in comparison with a PD-1 inhibitor plus
chemotherapy for patients with biomarker-defined, advanced NSCLC, the efficacy of
selpercatinib was superior in patients with RET fusion–positive NSCLC. The outcomes
in this trial highlight the importance of comprehensive genomic testing for RET fusions at
the time of diagnosis to inform first-line therapy for this patient population.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04194944 titled 'A Study of Selpercatinib (LY3527723) in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic RET Fusion-Positive Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis for Clinical Study NCT04194944 utilized the Kaplan–Meier method to estimate progression-free survival, overall survival, and time to confirmed worsening of symptoms. A stratified Cox regression model was employed to determine hazard ratios. The intention-to-treat population was the focus, with a gated testing strategy applied for multiplicity correction. This strategy involved testing first in the ITT-pembrolizumab subgroup before proceeding to the overall ITT population, with an interim alpha level of 0.012.
source_chunks: The Kaplan–Meier method was used to estimate medians and percentages of patients at various time points for each group for progression-free survival, overall survival, and the time to confirmed worsening of symptoms. Hazard ratios for time-to-event end points were estimated with a stratified Cox regression model.

Chunk 5: Zhou et al. Page 5
however, the sponsor did not review or analyze aggregate data and response assessments
or the assessments of disease progression conducted by blinded independent central review
and by the investigator in accordance with RECIST, version 1.1.13 Patients were allowed to

Chunk 6: This trial was designed jointly by the sponsor (Loxo Oncology, a wholly owned subsidiary
of Eli Lilly) and the investigators. The sponsor collected, analyzed, and interpreted the trial
data in collaboration with the authors. The authors provided input to revise the manuscript,
and writing assistance with the submitted manuscript was funded by the sponsor. The
authors vouch for the completeness and accuracy of the data and for the adherence of the
trial to the protocol.
Patients were randomly assigned to receive either selpercatinib (160 mg twice daily) in
continuous 21-day cycles or pemetrexed (500 mg per square meter of body-surface area)
with vitamin supplementation along with the investigator’s choice of platinum therapy
(carboplatin [area under the concentration–time curve, 5; maximum dose, 750 mg] or
cisplatin [75 mg per square meter]) with or without pembrolizumab (200 mg) every 21 days.
Patients were stratified according to geographic region (East Asia vs. elsewhere), status with
respect to brain metastases at baseline (absent or unknown vs. present), and whether the
investigator had intended (before randomization) to treat the patient with pembrolizumab
or without pembrolizumab. Initially, eligible patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio
to the selpercatinib group or the control group; however, on amendment of the protocol,
patients were randomly assigned in a 2:1 ratio to the selpercatinib group or the control
group, and therefore the final ratio of randomization was 1.6:1. After the completion of
four cycles of control treatment without progressive disease, patients in the control group
could continue to receive pemetrexed with or without pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab
was administered for a maximum of 35 cycles. Because of differences in treatment
administration between the groups, the trial was open label to patients and investigators;

Chunk 8: All analyses were conducted in accordance with the statistical analysis plan (available with
the protocol). We calculated that a total of 140 events of disease progression or death in
the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population would be required to achieve 89% overall
statistical power with a two-sided type 1 error of 0.05. The preplanned interim efficacy
analysis occurred after 98 events of disease progression assessed by blinded independent
central review or death in the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population, with data cutoff
on May 1, 2023. The nominal two-sided alpha level was fixed at 0.012 at the interim
analysis. With the gated testing strategy, progression-free survival in the intention-to-treat
population was tested conditionally on achievement of significance for progression-free
survival in the intention-to-treat–pembrolizumab population, which acted as a gatekeeper for
testing overall survival in the intention-to-treat population. Descriptive results of all other
analyses, including subgroup analyses, are reported as point estimates and 95% confidence
intervals; the confidence intervals in these analyses were not adjusted for multiplicity and
should not be used to infer definitive treatment effects. The Kaplan–Meier method was
used to estimate medians and percentages of patients at various time points for each group
for progression-free survival, overall survival, and the time to confirmed worsening of
symptoms. Hazard ratios for time-to-event end points were estimated with a stratified Cox
regression model. The assumption of proportionality was assessed graphically by evaluating
whether the estimated log-minus-log survival curves were parallel.

Chunk 11: This trial was designed jointly by the sponsor (Loxo Oncology, a wholly owned subsidiary
of Eli Lilly) and the investigators. The sponsor collected, analyzed, and interpreted the trial
data in collaboration with the authors. The authors provided input to revise the manuscript,
and writing assistance with the submitted manuscript was funded by the sponsor. The
authors vouch for the completeness and accuracy of the data and for the adherence of the
trial to the protocol.
Patients were randomly assigned to receive either selpercatinib (160 mg twice daily) in
continuous 21-day cycles or pemetrexed (500 mg per square meter of body-surface area)
with vitamin supplementation along with the investigator’s choice of platinum therapy
(carboplatin [area under the concentration–time curve, 5; maximum dose, 750 mg] or
cisplatin [75 mg per square meter]) with or without pembrolizumab (200 mg) every 21 days.
Patients were stratified according to geographic region (East Asia vs. elsewhere), status with
respect to brain metastases at baseline (absent or unknown vs. present), and whether the
investigator had intended (before randomization) to treat the patient with pembrolizumab
or without pembrolizumab. Initially, eligible patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio
to the selpercatinib group or the control group; however, on amendment of the protocol,
patients were randomly assigned in a 2:1 ratio to the selpercatinib group or the control
group, and therefore the final ratio of randomization was 1.6:1. After the completion of
four cycles of control treatment without progressive disease, patients in the control group
could continue to receive pemetrexed with or without pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab
was administered for a maximum of 35 cycles. Because of differences in treatment
administration between the groups, the trial was open label to patients and investigators;

question: For Clinical Study NCT03594747 titled 'A Study of Tislelizumab in Combination With Chemotherapy Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Lung Cancer': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: Participants in the clinical trial were required to have treatment-naive, histologically confirmed stage IIIB/IV squamous non-small cell lung cancer (sq-NSCLC). The exact age range is not mentioned in these contexts. Biomarkers mentioned include tumor programmed cell death 1 ligand 1 (PD-L1) expression, which was used for stratification but not as an inclusion criterion.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03594747 titled 'A Study of Tislelizumab in Combination With Chemotherapy Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Lung Cancer': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings for Clinical Study NCT03594747 included significantly improved IRC-assessed progression-free survival (PFS) with hazard ratios of 0.524 (95% CI, 0.370-0.742; P < .001) for arm A vs C and 0.478 (95% CI, 0.336-0.679; P < .001) for arm B vs C. IRC-assessed objective response rates (ORRs) were higher in arms A (72.5%) and B (74.8%) compared to C (49.6%). The IRC-assessed duration of response was longer in arms A (8.2 months) and B (8.6 months) vs C (4.2 months). No significant association was observed between PD-L1 expression and IRC-assessed PFS or ORR. The most common grade 3 or greater adverse event (AE) was decreased neutrophil levels, consistent with chemotherapy toxic effects. Six treatment-related AEs led to death, though none were solely attributed to tislelizumab.
source_chunks: After a median study follow-up of 8.6 months (95% CI, 8.1-9.0 months), IRC-assessed PFS was significantly improved with tislelizumab plus chemotherapy (arm A, 7.6 months; arm B, 7.6 months) vs chemotherapy alone (arm C, 5.5 months; hazard ratios were 0.524 (95% CI, 0.370-0.742; P < .001 [A vs C]) and 0.478 (95% CI, 0.336-0.679; P < .001 [B vs C]).
Higher IRC-assessed ORR and longer IRC-assessed duration of response were observed in arms A (72.5%; 8.2 months) and B (74.8%; 8.6 months) vs C (49.6%; 4.2 months).
No association was observed between PD-L1 expression and IRC-assessed PFS or ORR.
In each arm, the most common grade of 3 or greater AE was decreased neutrophil levels, which aligned with known chemotherapy toxic effects.
Six treatment-related AEs leading to death occurred; however, no deaths were solely attributed to tislelizumab.
Chunk 6: ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT03594747.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03594747 titled 'A Study of Tislelizumab in Combination With Chemotherapy Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Lung Cancer': What were the most common adverse events (grade 3+), their incidence rates by arm, and any serious events leading to discontinuation
ground_truth_answer: The most common grade 3 or greater adverse event (AE) across all arms was decreased neutrophil levels. In arm A, 12.5% of patients experienced this AE, in arm B it was 29.7%, and in arm C it was 15.4%. Discontinuation of treatment due to AEs occurred in 15 patients (12.5%) in arm A, 35 patients (29.7%) in arm B, and 18 patients (15.4%) in arm C. There were six treatment-related AEs leading to death, though none were solely attributed to tislelizumab.
source_chunks: In each arm, the most common grade of 3 or greater AE was decreased neutrophil levels, which aligned with known chemotherapy toxic effects.
Discontinuation of any treatment because of AEs was reported in 15 (12.5%; arm A), 35 (29.7%; arm B), and 18 (15.4%; arm C) patients.
Six treatment-related AEs leading to death occurred; however, no deaths were solely attributed to tislelizumab.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03663205 titled 'A Study Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Tislelizumab Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Non-Squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial included patients with advanced nonsquamous NSCLC, specifically those with adenocarcinoma histology. The age range of participants was from 18 to 75 years, with a median age of 61 years. The disease stage at baseline included both Stage IIIB and Stage IV NSCLC. Additional inclusion criteria required patients to have measurable disease, an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 0 or 1, and to be treatment-naive. Patients were also eligible if they did not have known sensitizing EGFR mutations or ALK rearrangements. No specific required biomarkers beyond histological type and disease stage were necessary for inclusion.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03663205 titled 'A Study Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Tislelizumab Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Non-Squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
ground_truth_answer: The trial was randomized, controlled, open-label (not blinded), and parallel with a crossover option. The sample size was 223 patients in Arm A and 111 patients in Arm B.
source_chunks: Between July 23, 2018, and July 31, 2019, a total of 334 eligible patients were randomized to either arm A (n=223) or arm B (n=111)(Fig.1).
Patients in arm B could cross over to receive tislelizumab monotherapy on independent review committee (IRC)-confirmed disease progression.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03663205 titled 'A Study Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Tislelizumab Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Non-Squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the study was progression-free survival (PFS) as assessed by the Independent Review Committee (IRC). It was measured using a stratified log-rank test and a stratified Cox proportional hazard model. The predefined success threshold was a one-sided p-value of 0.0078.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: The primary end point of this study was the comparison of PFS as assessed by the IRC (PFS ) between tislelizumab plus carboplatin or cisplatin in combination with pemetrexed (arm A) versus carboplatin or cisplatin in combination with pemetrexed alone (arm B) in all randomized patients (intent-to-treat [ITT]).

Chunk 1: Between-group differences in PFS were assessed using the stratified log-rank test. HRs and associated 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated using a stratified Cox proportional hazard model with Efron’s method for handling tied events.
Chunk 1: prespecified efficacy boundaries were one-sided (p=0.0078).

question: For Clinical Study NCT03663205 titled 'A Study Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Tislelizumab Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Non-Squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
ground_truth_answer: The secondary efficacy endpoints assessed in the study included progression-free survival (PFS) as assessed by the investigator, PFS according to PD-L1 expression, objective response rate (ORR) as assessed by the IRC, duration of response (DoR), and overall survival (OS). No exploratory endpoints such as quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes were mentioned in these contexts.
source_chunks: Key secondary efficacy end points assessed in the ITT analysis set included PFS as assessed by the investigator, PFS according to PD-L1 expression, objective response rate (ORR) assessed by the IRC, duration of response as assessed by the IRC (DoR ), and overall survival (OS).

question: For Clinical Study NCT03663205 titled 'A Study Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Tislelizumab Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Non-Squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the study was progression-free survival (PFS) as assessed by the Independent Review Committee (IRC). The results showed a hazard ratio (HR) of 0.645 with a 95% confidence interval (CI), indicating that the primary objective was met with significantly prolonged PFS in the tislelizumab arm compared to chemotherapy alone. The p-value was not mentioned in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Chunk 2: The primary end point of this study was the comparison of PFS as assessed by the IRC (PFS ) between tislelizumab plus carboplatin or cisplatin in combination with pemetrexed (arm A) versus carboplatin or cisplatin in combination with pemetrexed alone (arm B) in all randomized patients (intent-to-treat [ITT]).

Chunk 3: The primary end point of this study was the comparison of PFS as assessed by the IRC (PFS ) between tislelizumab plus carboplatin or cisplatin in combination with pemetrexed (arm A) versus carboplatin or cisplatin in combination with pemetrexed alone (arm B) in all randomized patients (intent-to-treat [ITT]).

question: For Clinical Study NCT03663205 titled 'A Study Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Tislelizumab Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Non-Squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of Progression-Free Survival (PFS) as assessed by the Independent Review Committee (IRC) showed a hazard ratio (HR) of 0.64 with a 95% confidence interval (CI) of 0.50-0.82. Secondary endpoints included PFS as assessed by the investigator, PFS according to PD-L1 expression, objective response rate (ORR), and duration of response. The ORR by IRC was 54.3% for partial response (PR) and 3.1% for complete response (CR). The HR for PFS according to PD-L1 expression was 0.645. However, the HR for PFS as assessed by the investigator and the median duration of response were not mentioned in the provided contexts. No significant adjustments were noted beyond the stratified log-rank test and Cox proportional hazard model with Efron’s method for tied events.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: Between-group differences in PFS were assessed using the stratified log-rank test. HRs and associated 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated using a stratified Cox proportional hazard model with Efron’s method for handling tied events.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03663205 titled 'A Study Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Tislelizumab Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Non-Squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the most common adverse events (grade 3+), their incidence rates by arm, and any serious events leading to discontinuation
ground_truth_answer: The most common adverse events (grade 3+) in Arm A (Tislelizumab + PP) were neutropenia (44.6%), leukopenia (21.6%), anemia (14.9%), and thrombocytopenia (19.4%). In Arm B (PP alone), the most common were neutropenia (35.5%), leukopenia (14.5%), anemia (11.8%), and thrombocytopenia (13.6%). Serious TEAEs were reported in 33.3% of Arm A, but no specific serious events leading to discontinuation were detailed in the provided contexts.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03663205 titled 'A Study Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Tislelizumab Versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Non-Squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis used the stratified log-rank test to assess between-group differences in PFS. Hazard ratios (HRs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated using a stratified Cox proportional hazard model with Efron’s method for tied events. A posthoc PFS interaction analysis was performed using a Cox model to estimate HR, including covariates such as treatment and PD-L1 expression. The interim analysis used a one-sided p-value of 0.0078 with multiplicity adjustments.
source_chunks: Between-group differences in PFS were assessed using the stratified log-rank test. HRs and associated 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated using a stratified Cox proportional hazard model with Efron’s method for handling tied events.
A posthoc PFS interaction analysis for treatment and PD-L1 expression subgroups was performed, using a Cox model to estimate HR.
One protocol-specified interim analysis was to be conducted after approximately 153 events; prespecified efficacy boundaries were one-sided (p=0.0078).

ground_truth_answer: There is no evidence in the provided contexts that this clinical study incorporated AI innovations such as AI-driven patient recruitment, AI-drug discovery, predictive modeling for outcomes, or image analysis for endpoints. The study utilized traditional methods for tumor assessments, statistical analyses, and adverse event monitoring.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04035486 titled 'A Study of Osimertinib With or Without Chemotherapy as 1st Line Treatment in Patients With Mutated Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (FLAURA2)': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: Participants in the clinical trial NCT04035486 were required to have measurable or non-measurable CNS lesions at baseline per CNS BICR using RECIST 1.1. Baseline brain imaging (MRI or CT) was mandatory unless clinically indicated. Measurable lesions were defined as being at least 10 mm or twice the slice thickness, while non-measurable lesions included leptomeningeal disease. Prior radiotherapy sites were considered non-target. The age range and disease stage details were not mentioned in the provided contexts. Required biomarkers included mutated epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) status, as the study focused on patients with mutated EGFR non-small cell lung cancer.
source_chunks: Patients with ≥one measurable and/or nonmeasurable baseline CNS lesion by neuroradiologist CNS BICR were included in the CNS full analysis set (cFAS); the CNS evaluable-for-response set (cEFR) included only those with ≥one measurable target CNS lesion.

metastases, no scheduled brain imaging was required, unless clinically indicated and/or until disease progression RESULTS by RECIST 1.1 per investigator assessment. All CNS scans were evaluated by independent neuroradiologist review Patients (CNS BICR) per modified RECIST 1.1 guidelines.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04035486 titled 'A Study of Osimertinib With or Without Chemotherapy as 1st Line Treatment in Patients With Mutated Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (FLAURA2)': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings for Clinical Study NCT04035486, titled 'A Study of Osimertinib With or Without Chemotherapy as 1st Line Treatment in Patients With Mutated Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (FLAURA2),' include a hazard ratio of 0.40 (95% CI: 0.19 to 0.84) for progression-free survival, indicating a significant improvement. Additionally, the odds ratio for CNS best objective response was 0.70 (95% CI: 0.26 to 1.88). No specific adjustments to the endpoints were noted in the provided contexts.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04035486 titled 'A Study of Osimertinib With or Without Chemotherapy as 1st Line Treatment in Patients With Mutated Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (FLAURA2)': What were the most common adverse events (grade 3+), their incidence rates by arm, and any serious events leading to discontinuation
ground_truth_answer: The most common adverse events (grade 3+) in the Osimertinib Plus Platinum-Pemetrexed arm occurred in 75(64%) of patients, while in the Osimertinib Monotherapy arm, they occurred in 30(29%) of patients. The incidence rates for grade 3+ adverse events were higher in the combination arm compared to the monotherapy arm. Serious events leading to discontinuation were more frequent in the combination arm, with 53(45%) of patients discontinuing treatment, primarily due to chemotherapy-related adverse events, whereas in the monotherapy arm, 6(6%) discontinued due to osimertinib-related events.
source_chunks: Chunk 2: TABLE A4. Adverse Events in the cFAS and the Overall FLAURA2 Population. Any AE of grade ≥3: 75(64) for Osimertinib Plus Platinum-Pemetrexed and 30(29) for Osimertinib Monotherapy.
Chunk 2: AEs leading to treatment discontinuation: 53(45) in the combination arm and 6(6) in the monotherapy arm.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04035486 titled 'A Study of Osimertinib With or Without Chemotherapy as 1st Line Treatment in Patients With Mutated Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (FLAURA2)': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis for Clinical Study NCT04035486 (FLAURA2) utilized Kaplan-Meier methodology to assess CNS progression-free survival (PFS). Both stratified (using the cFAS) and unstratified (using the cEFR) log-rank tests were employed for comparisons. Additionally, logistic regression was applied to evaluate CNS objective response rate (ORR) and disease control rate (DCR). However, there was no explicit mention of multiplicity corrections in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Chunk 3: CNSPFSwasevaluatedusingKaplan-Meiermethodology and analyzed by a stratified (cFAS;by race, WHO PS, and methodusedfortissuetesting)orunstratified(cEFR)log- rank test.
Chunk 3: CNSORRandCNSDCRbyCNSBICR were analyzed using logistic regression with a factor for treatment.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03703297 titled 'Study of Durvalumab + Tremelimumab, Durvalumab, and Placebo in Limited Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer in Patients Who Have Not Progressed Following Concurrent Chemoradiation Therapy': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer:
The inclusion criteria for participants in the clinical trial NCT03703297 included patients aged 18 years or older with limited-stage small-cell lung cancer (LS-SCLC) stages I-III, where stages I or II must be medically inoperable. Patients must have an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 0 or 1 at both enrollment and randomization. They must have received first-line concurrent chemoradiotherapy (cCRT) with four cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy and intravenous etoposide, concurrent with radiotherapy (RT) administered according to local standards. The RT must have been initiated no later than the end of cycle 2 of chemotherapy and consisted of either 60-66 Gy over 6 weeks (standard once-daily schedule) or 45 Gy over 3 weeks (hyperfractionated twice-daily schedule). Patients who achieved disease control after three cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy were permitted if no additional benefit was expected from a fourth cycle, as determined by the investigator. 

Patients must have achieved a complete response (CR), partial response (PR), or stable disease (SD) without progressive disease (PD) after definitive, platinum-based cCRT. Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) was optional but had to be conducted within 1-42 days before randomization and the first dose of the investigational product. 

Additional requirements included availability of tumor tissue for analysis, adequate organ and bone marrow function, and an estimated life expectancy of at least 12 weeks. No specific biomarkers were explicitly required for inclusion beyond PD-L1 expression, which was part of exploratory analyses.
source_chunks: Written informed consent; Age ≥18 years at screening; Documented histologic or cytologic evidence of LS-SCLC (stage I-III; stage I or II must be medically inoperable); WHO/ECOG performance status of 0 or 1 at enrollment and randomization; Received first-line cCRT (as defined); Received 4 cycles of chemotherapy containing platinum and IV etoposide concurrent with RT administered as per local standard of care regimens, which must be completed within 1-42 days before randomization and the first dose of investigational product; Received a total dose of RT of 60-66 Gy over 6 wk for standard QD RT schedules or 45 Gy over 3 wk for hyperfractionated BID RT schedules; RT must have begun no later than the end of cycle 2 of chemotherapy; Three cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy concurrent with RT is permitted if disease control was achieved and no additional benefit can be expected from an additional cycle of chemotherapy as determined by the investigator; Patients must have achieved CR, PR, or SD, without PD after definitive, platinum-based cCRT; PCI can be delivered per investigator discretion and local standard of care and must be conducted after cCRT has completed and within 1-42 days before randomization and first dose of investigational product; Availability of tumor tissue; Adequate organ and bone marrow function and estimated life expectancy of ≥12 wk.
The baseline demographic data, AEs, and HRQoL and function improvement rates will be summarized using descriptive statistics.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03703297 titled 'Study of Durvalumab + Tremelimumab, Durvalumab, and Placebo in Limited Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer in Patients Who Have Not Progressed Following Concurrent Chemoradiation Therapy': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
ground_truth_answer: The exclusion criteria for the study include: Mixed SCLC and NSCLC histologic features; Extensive-stage SCLC; Received regimens other than etoposide and platinum as consolidation chemotherapy after RT completion; Received sequential chemoradiotherapy for LS-SCLC; PD during cCRT; Patients with any history of grade 2 pneumonitis (except for resolved infective pneumonitis); Active or previous documented autoimmune or inflammatory disorders or uncontrolled intercurrent illness; History of another primary malignancy, leptomeningeal carcinomatosis, or primary immunodeficiency, or active infection; Any unresolved toxicity (CTCAE grade 2) from previous chemoradiotherapy; Active infection, including tuberculosis, HIV, hepatitis B and C.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03703297 titled 'Study of Durvalumab + Tremelimumab, Durvalumab, and Placebo in Limited Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer in Patients Who Have Not Progressed Following Concurrent Chemoradiation Therapy': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
ground_truth_answer: The trial was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel study with three arms. The sample size per arm was approximately 200 participants.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial
Chunk 2: phase 3, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial
Chunk 4: Durvalumab 1500 mg + placebo tremelimumab q4w for four doses, followed by durvalumab 1500 mg q4w (n~200)
Chunk 5: Durvalumab 1500 mg + placebo tremelimumab q4w for four doses, followed by durvalumab 1500 mg q4w (n~200)

question: For Clinical Study NCT03703297 titled 'Study of Durvalumab + Tremelimumab, Durvalumab, and Placebo in Limited Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer in Patients Who Have Not Progressed Following Concurrent Chemoradiation Therapy': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoints of the study were progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS). PFS was measured by blinded independent central review according to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors (RECIST) version 1.1. The predefined success threshold for these endpoints was not mentioned in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: The primary endpoints are progression-free survival (PFS), assessed by blinded independent central review per RECIST v1.1, and OS.

Chunk 5: In a phase 3, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial, we assigned patients to receive durvalumab at a dose of 1500 mg, durvalumab (1500 mg) plus tremelimumab at a dose of 75 mg (four doses only), or placebo every 4 weeks for up to 24 months. Randomization was stratified according to disease stage (I or II vs. III) and receipt of prophylactic cranial irradiation (yes vs. no). Results of the first planned interim analysis of the two primary end points of overall survival and progression-free survival (assessed on the basis of blinded independent central review according to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, version 1.1) with durvalumab as compared with placebo (data cutoff date, January 15, 2024) are reported; results in the durvalumab-tremelimumab group remain blinded.

Chunk 6: Adjuvant therapy with durvalumab led to significantly longer overall survival and progression-free survival than placebo among patients with limited-stage small-cell lung cancer. (Funded by AstraZeneca; ADRIATIC ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT03703297.).

question: For Clinical Study NCT03703297 titled 'Study of Durvalumab + Tremelimumab, Durvalumab, and Placebo in Limited Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer in Patients Who Have Not Progressed Following Concurrent Chemoradiation Therapy': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the study include overall survival (OS) at 24 and 36 months, progression-free survival (PFS) at 18 and 24 months, objective response rate (ORR), time to death or distant metastasis (TTDM), time from randomization to second progression, safety, and tolerability. Additional secondary endpoints include symptoms and health-related quality of life (HRQoL) using the QLQ-C30 and QLQ-LC13 questionnaires. Exploratory endpoints involve biomarker analyses such as PD-L1 expression and tumor mutational burden.
source_chunks: The secondary endpoints include OS at 24 and 36 months, PFS at 18 and 24 months, objective response rate, time to death or distant metastasis (TTDM), time from randomization to second progression, and safety and tolerability. Additionalsecondaryendpointsinclude symptoms and health-related quality of life (HRQoL) using the QLQ-C30, version 3, and QLQ-LC13 questionnaires.
Tissue samples will be collected for exploratory analyses of, but not limited to, PD-L1 expression and the tumor mutational burden.

Chunk 6: In a phase 3, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial, we assigned patients to receive durvalumab at a dose of 1500 mg, durvalumab (1500 mg) plus tremelimumab at a dose of 75 mg (four doses only), or placebo every 4 weeks for up to 24 months. Randomization was stratified according to disease stage (I or II vs. III) and receipt of prophylactic cranial irradiation (yes vs. no). Results of the first planned interim analysis of the two primary end points of overall survival and progression-free survival (assessed on the basis of blinded independent central review according to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, version 1.1) with durvalumab as compared with placebo (data cutoff date, January 15, 2024) are reported; results in the durvalumab-tremelimumab group remain blinded.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03703297 titled 'Study of Durvalumab + Tremelimumab, Durvalumab, and Placebo in Limited Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer in Patients Who Have Not Progressed Following Concurrent Chemoradiation Therapy': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoints of the study were progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS), assessed by blinded independent central review according to RECIST v1.1. However, the specific point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met were not mentioned in these contexts.
source_chunks: Chunk 2: ...primary end points of overall survival and progression-free survival (assessed on the basis of blinded independent central review according to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, version 1.1) with durvalumab as compared with placebo...
Chunk 3: The primary endpoints are progression-free survival (PFS), assessed by blinded independent central review per RECIST v1.1, and OS.

Chunk 3: In a phase 3, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial, we assigned patients to receive durvalumab at a dose of 1500 mg, durvalumab (1500 mg) plus tremelimumab at a dose of 75 mg (four doses only), or placebo every 4 weeks for up to 24 months. Randomization was stratified according to disease stage (I or II vs. III) and receipt of prophylactic cranial irradiation (yes vs. no). Results of the first planned interim analysis of the two primary end points of overall survival and progression-free survival (assessed on the basis of blinded independent central review according to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, version 1.1) with durvalumab as compared with placebo (data cutoff date, January 15, 2024) are reported; results in the durvalumab-tremelimumab group remain blinded.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03703297 titled 'Study of Durvalumab + Tremelimumab, Durvalumab, and Placebo in Limited Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer in Patients Who Have Not Progressed Following Concurrent Chemoradiation Therapy': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints for the ADRIATIC study (NCT03703297) include overall survival (OS) at 24 and 36 months, progression-free survival (PFS) at 18 and 24 months, objective response rate (ORR), time to death or distant metastasis (TTDM), time from randomization to second progression, and symptoms and health-related quality of life (HRQoL) using the European Organization for Research and Treatment of Cancer quality-of-life questionnaires. No specific hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences for these secondary endpoints are provided in the retrieved contexts.
