Chunk 5: In a phase 3, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial, we assigned patients to receive durvalumab at a dose of 1500 mg, durvalumab (1500 mg) plus tremelimumab at a dose of 75 mg (four doses only), or placebo every 4 weeks for up to 24 months. Randomization was stratified according to disease stage (I or II vs. III) and receipt of prophylactic cranial irradiation (yes vs. no). Results of the first planned interim analysis of the two primary end points of overall survival and progression-free survival (assessed on the basis of blinded independent central review according to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, version 1.1) with durvalumab as compared with placebo (data cutoff date, January 15, 2024) are reported; results in the durvalumab-tremelimumab group remain blinded.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03703297 titled 'Study of Durvalumab + Tremelimumab, Durvalumab, and Placebo in Limited Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer in Patients Who Have Not Progressed Following Concurrent Chemoradiation Therapy': What were the most common adverse events (grade 3+), their incidence rates by arm, and any serious events leading to discontinuation
ground_truth_answer: The most common adverse events (grade 3 or higher) in the durvalumab group were reported in 24.4% of patients, while in the placebo group, 24.2% experienced such events. Pneumonitis or radiation pneumonitis occurred in 3.1% of durvalumab patients and 2.6% of placebo patients. Adverse events leading to discontinuation were seen in 16.4% of durvalumab patients and 10.6% of placebo patients. Deaths due to adverse events occurred in 2.7% of durvalumab patients and 1.9% of placebo patients.
source_chunks: The incidence of adverse events with a maximum grade of 3 or 4 was 24.4% among patients receiving durvalumab and 24.2% among patients receiving placebo; adverse events led to discontinuation in 16.4% and 10.6% of the patients, respectively, and led to death in 2.7% and 1.9%. Pneumonitis or radiation pneumonitis with a maximum grade of 3 or 4 occurred in 3.1% of the patients in the durvalumab group and in 2.6% of those in the placebo group.

Chunk 2: A total of 264 patients were assigned to the durvalumab group, 200 to the durvalumab-tremelimumab group, and 266 to the placebo group. Durvalumab therapy led to significantly longer overall survival than placebo (median, 55.9 months [95% confidence interval {CI}, 37.3 to not reached] vs. 33.4 months [95% CI, 25.5 to 39.9]; hazard ratio for death, 0.73; 98.321% CI, 0.54 to 0.98; P = 0.01), as well as to significantly longer progression-free survival (median 16.6 months [95% CI, 10.2 to 28.2] vs. 9.2 months [95% CI, 7.4 to 12.9]; hazard ratio for progression or death, 0.76; 97.195% CI, 0.59 to 0.98; P = 0.02). The incidence of adverse events with a maximum grade of 3 or 4 was 24.4% among patients receiving durvalumab and 24.2% among patients receiving placebo; adverse events led to discontinuation in 16.4% and 10.6% of the patients, respectively, and led to death in 2.7% and 1.9%. Pneumonitis or radiation pneumonitis with a maximum grade of 3 or 4 occurred in 3.1% of the patients in the durvalumab group and in 2.6% of those in the placebo group.

Chunk 8: In a phase 3, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial, we assigned patients to receive durvalumab at a dose of 1500 mg, durvalumab (1500 mg) plus tremelimumab at a dose of 75 mg (four doses only), or placebo every 4 weeks for up to 24 months. Randomization was stratified according to disease stage (I or II vs. III) and receipt of prophylactic cranial irradiation (yes vs. no). Results of the first planned interim analysis of the two primary end points of overall survival and progression-free survival (assessed on the basis of blinded independent central review according to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, version 1.1) with durvalumab as compared with placebo (data cutoff date, January 15, 2024) are reported; results in the durvalumab-tremelimumab group remain blinded.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03515837 titled 'Study of Pemetrexed + Platinum Chemotherapy With or Without Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) in Adults With Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor- (TKI)-Resistant Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor- (EGFR)-Mutated Metastatic Non-squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-3475-789/KEYNOTE-789)': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in the clinical trial NCT03515837 included adults with Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor (TKI)-Resistant Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor (EGFR)-Mutated Metastatic Non-squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC). The age range of participants was 34 to 87 years, with a median age of 62 years. Patients were required to have metastatic disease and specific EGFR-activating mutations, such as L858R or DEL19. Additionally, participants could have stable brain metastases if radiologically confirmed. The Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status was primarily 0 or 1, indicating relatively good health. PD-L1 tumor proportion score (TPS) was assessed but was not an exclusion criterion. Required biomarkers included EGFR mutation status, specifically L858R or DEL19.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03515837 titled 'Study of Pemetrexed + Platinum Chemotherapy With or Without Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) in Adults With Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor- (TKI)-Resistant Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor- (EGFR)-Mutated Metastatic Non-squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-3475-789/KEYNOTE-789)': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the study was Progression-Free Survival (PFS). It was measured using the Lan-DeMets O’Brien-Fleming spending function with predefined efficacy boundaries. The predefined success threshold was determined based on the actual number of events observed.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: On the basis of the actual number of events observed, the efficacy boundaries were P5.0117 for PFS (at IA2) and OS (at FA) per the protocol-specified minimum spending approach.
Chunk 1: All superiority boundaries were calculated using the Lan-DeMets O’Brien-Fleming spending function.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03515837 titled 'Study of Pemetrexed + Platinum Chemotherapy With or Without Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) in Adults With Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor- (TKI)-Resistant Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor- (EGFR)-Mutated Metastatic Non-squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-3475-789/KEYNOTE-789)': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
ground_truth_answer:
The secondary endpoints assessed for Clinical Study NCT03515837 were not mentioned in the provided contexts. Therefore, the information is not available based on the given expert notes. 

Answer: N/A. Secondary endpoints such as quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes were not mentioned in these contexts.
source_chunks: Secondary endpoints Assessments included objective response rate (ORR; proportion of patients with complete response [CR] or partial response [PR]), duration of response (DOR; time from evidence of response to first of PD or death in patients with CR or PR), PROs, and safety.

The protocol-specified secondary PRO end points included meanscore change from baseline to week 18 in the global health status (GHS)/quality of life (QoL) scale (GHS item 29; QoL item 30) and the time to true deterioration (TTD; time from baseline to first onset of ≥10 points of deterioration from baseline, with confirmed second adjacent deterioration of ≥10 points from baseline in any of the three symptoms) in the composite end point of cough (QLQ-LC13 item 1), chest pain (QLQ-LC13 item 10), or dyspnea (QLQ-C30 item 8).

question: For Clinical Study NCT03515837 titled 'Study of Pemetrexed + Platinum Chemotherapy With or Without Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) in Adults With Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor- (TKI)-Resistant Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor- (EGFR)-Mutated Metastatic Non-squamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-3475-789/KEYNOTE-789)': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis for Clinical Study NCT03515837 utilized the graphical method of Maurer and Bretz to maintain a family-wise type I error rate of 0.025 (one-sided) for testing PFS, OS, and ORR, with preallocated alpha levels of 0.0125, 0.0125, and 0, respectively. The study employed the Lan-DeMets O'Brien-Fleming spending function for adaptive boundary adjustments. Kaplan-Meier estimates were used for time-to-event endpoints, and Cox regression was likely employed for hazard ratios. The analysis was conducted in the intention-to-treat (ITT) population.
source_chunks: The graphical method of Maurer and Bretz was used to control the family-wise type I error rate at 0.025 (one-sided) for hypothesis testing of PFS, OS, and ORR, with preallocated alpha at .0125, .0125, and 0, respectively.
The actual boundaries were updated on the basis of the actual number of events observed and calculated using the Lan-DeMets O’Brien-Flemingspendingfunction.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03829332 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007)': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: Participants in the clinical trial NCT03829332 were required to have PD-L1-positive, treatment-naïve, locally advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). The age range for participants was 34 to 85 years. The required biomarker was PD-L1 positivity with a tumor proportion score (TPS) of at least 1%.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03829332 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007)': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
ground_truth_answer: Exclusion criteria for the study included: history of gastrointestinal conditions affecting lenvatinib absorption; clinically relevant cardiovascular impairment (e.g., congestive heart failure, unstable angina, myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or arrhythmia) within 12 months; prior anti-PD-1/PD-L1/PD-L2 therapy or any treatment directed at another stimulatory or co-inhibitory T-cell receptor, or prior lenvatinib treatment (as monotherapy or in combination with anti-PD-1 agents); recent radiotherapy (within 14 days or >30 Gy within 6 months); radiographic evidence of major blood vessel invasion or intratumoral cavitation; clinically important hemoptysis (defined as 0.5 teaspoon of bright red blood); central nervous system metastases or carcinomatous meningitis unless previously treated and stable; active autoimmune disease requiring systemic treatment; history of non-infectious pneumonitis requiring steroids or current pneumonitis/interstitial lung disease; immunodeficiency or immunosuppressive therapy within 7 days before treatment.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03829332 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007)': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
ground_truth_answer: The trial was a randomized, double-blind, controlled, parallel study. The sample size per arm was approximately 310 patients.
source_chunks: In this multicenter, double-blind, phase 3 study, patients were randomized 1:1 to receive either oral lenvatinib 20 mg or placebo administered once daily; all patients received intravenous pembrolizumab 200 mg administered once every 3 weeks for up to 35 cycles.
Planned enrollment for the global study was 620 patients.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03829332 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007)': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoints of the study were overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS). OS was measured as the time from randomization to death due to any cause, while PFS was measured as the time from randomization to the first documented assessment of progressive disease (PD) per RECIST version 1.1. The predefined success thresholds were based on hazard ratios (HR) with specific alpha levels: for OS, a HR of 0.71 with a one-sided alpha of 1.95%, and for PFS, a HR of 0.7 with a one-sided alpha of 0.55%.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: The study had two primary end points: overall survival (OS), defined as time from randomization to death because of any cause, and progression-free survival (PFS), defined as time from randomization to the first documented assessment of PD (per RECIST version 1.1).

Chunk 2: The study had two primary end points: overall survival (OS), defined as time from randomization to death because of any cause, and progression-free survival (PFS), defined as time from randomization to the first documented assessment of PD (per RECIST version 1.1).

ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the study included Objective Response Rate (ORR) and safety. An exploratory endpoint was the duration of response, defined as the time from the earliest documented response to the first occurrence of disease progression or death.
source_chunks: Secondary end points were ORR, defined as a confirmed complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) per RECIST version 1.1 by BICR, and safety. question: For Clinical Study NCT03829332 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007)': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoints for the study were Overall Survival (OS) and Progression-Free Survival (PFS). The median OS was 14.1 months (95% CI 11.4–19.0) in the lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab group and 16.4 months (95% CI 12.6–20.6) in the placebo plus pembrolizumab group, with a hazard ratio (HR) of 1.10 (95% CI 0.87–1.39) and a p-value of 0.797. The futility criterion for OS was met, indicating the primary objective for OS was not met. For PFS, the median was 6.6 months (95% CI 6.1–8.2) in the lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab group and 4.2 months (95% CI 4.1–6.2) in the placebo plus pembrolizumab group, with an HR of 0.78 (95% CI 0.64–0.95) and a nominal p-value of 0.00624. The futility criterion for PFS was not met, suggesting some benefit but not meeting the primary objective criteria.

Chunk 1: The HR for PFS was 0.71, indicating a treatment difference.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03829332 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007)': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings include a hazard ratio (HR) for disease progression or death of 1.18 (95% CI, 0.61-2.25) with a nominal p-value of 0.00624. The median progression-free survival (PFS) was 6.1 months (95% CI: 4.2–8.7) in the lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab group and 10.3 months (95% CI: 5.6–NR) in the placebo plus pembrolizumab group. The overall response rate (ORR) was evaluated as a secondary endpoint but specific values were not mentioned. Safety endpoints included adverse events of special interest, such as hypothyroidism (41.4% vs. 0%), hyperthyroidism (9.7% vs. 0.6%), pneumonitis (7.8% vs. 6.7%), and others, with varying frequencies between treatment groups. Significant adjustments included the reallocation of alpha levels for primary endpoints and a futility analysis that impacted the continuation of the study. The futility criterion for OS was met, leading to no further statistical testing.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: The hazard ratio (HR) of 0.71 and one-sided alpha of 1.95% for OS, and HR of 0.7 with one-sided alpha of 0.55% for PFS.
Chunk 2: Secondary end points were ORR, defined as a confirmed complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) per RECIST version 1.1 by BICR, and safety.
Chunk 4: Hazard ratio for disease progression or death, 0.78 (95% CI, 0.64-0.95) nominal P= 0.00624.

Chunk 5: Median OS was 11.4 months (95% CI: 8.4 mo–not reached [NR]) in the lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab group and NR in the placebo plus pembrolizumab group (HR, 1.53 [95% CI: 0.61–3.87]); median (95% CI) PFS was 6.1 (4.2–8.7) versus 10.3 (5.6–NR) months (HR ¼ 1.18 [95% CI: 0.61–2.25])

question: For Clinical Study NCT03829332 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007)': What were the most common adverse events (grade 3+), their incidence rates by arm, and any serious events leading to discontinuation
ground_truth_answer: The most common adverse events (grade 3+) in the lenvatinib + pembrolizumab arm included hypertension (17.2%), proteinuria (6.1%), diarrhea (3.2%), decreased appetite (1.9%), nausea (1.3%), increased ALT (2.6%), increased AST (1.9%), stomatitis (1.9%), fatigue (3.2%), asthenia (1.9%), rash (0.3%), PPES (1.9%), and decreased weight (1.9%). In the placebo + pembrolizumab arm, the most common grade 3+ adverse events included hypertension (2.6%), proteinuria (1.0%), diarrhea (0.6%), decreased appetite (0.3%), nausea (0%), increased ALT (2.2%), increased AST (1.6%), stomatitis (0%), fatigue (1.3%), asthenia (1.3%), rash (0.3%), PPES (0%), and decreased weight (0.3%). Serious events leading to discontinuation of any treatment occurred in 35 patients (11.3%) in the lenvatinib + pembrolizumab group and 17 patients (5.4%) in the placebo + pembrolizumab group.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03829332 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) (MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007)': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis used Kaplan-Meier estimates for OS and PFS, stratified log-rank tests for between-group differences, and stratified Cox proportional hazard models with Efron’s method for tie handling. The intention-to-treat population was analyzed. Multiplicity corrections included reallocating alpha levels from the secondary endpoint ORR to the primary endpoints PFS and OS.
source_chunks: Efficacy analyses were performed in all randomized patients according to assigned treatment (intention-to-treat population). Safety analyses were performed in all randomized patients who received at least one dose of the study treatment according to treatment received (as-treated population). OS and PFS were estimated using the nonparametric Kaplan-Meier method. For the OS analysis, patients without documented death were censored at the last known contact before the data cutoff date. For the PFS analysis, patients without documented PD or death were censored at the time of the last disease assessment before the data cutoff date or at the start of new anticancer therapy, whichever was earlier. Between-group treatment differences in OS and PFS were assessed using the stratified log-rank test; all p-values are one-sided. The magnitude of the treatment difference was calculated using a stratified Cox proportional hazard model with Efron’s method of tie handling.

In March 2021, the protocol was amended to allow a sufficient duration of follow-up for the evaluation of efficacy, and the alpha initially assigned to the secondary end point of ORR (one-sided alpha = 0.001), which was to be formally tested only at the first interim analysis (after approximately 420 patients had 9 months of follow-up), was reallocated to the primary end points of PFS and OS, with analysis planned after approximately 416 PFS events had occurred and the last patient randomized had approximately 8.8 months of follow-up.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05091567 titled 'A Phase III, Open-Label Study of Maintenance Lurbinectedin in Combination With Atezolizumab Compared With Atezolizumab in Participants With Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: Participants were aged 18 years or older with treatment-naive extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC). The exact inclusion criteria for biomarkers are not mentioned in these contexts.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: Eligible patients were aged 18 years or older with treatment-naive ES-SCLC.

Chunk 2: IMforte was a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial done at 96 hospitals and medical centres in 13 countries (Belgium, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Italy, Mexico, Poland, South Korea, Spain, Taiwan, Türkiye, the UK, and the USA). Eligible patients were aged 18 years or older with treatment-naive ES-SCLC. Patients received four 21-day cycles of induction treatment (atezolizumab, carboplatin, and etoposide). After completing induction treatment, eligible patients without disease progression were randomly assigned (1:1) using permuted blocks (Interactive Voice/Web Response System) to receive maintenance treatment intravenously every 3 weeks with lurbinectedin (3·2 mg/m2; with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor prophylaxis) plus atezolizumab (1200 mg) or atezolizumab (1200 mg). The two primary endpoints were independent review facility-assessed (IRF) progression-free survival and overall survival, measured from randomisation into the maintenance phase. Efficacy endpoints were assessed in the full analysis set, which included all patients who were randomly assigned to maintenance phase treatment, regardless of whether they received their assigned study treatment. Safety was assessed in all patients who received at least one dose of lurbinectedin or atezolizumab, and was analysed according to the treatment received. This study is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT05091567, and is closed for recruitment.

Chunk 3: Despite improved efficacy with first-line immune checkpoint inhibitors plus platinum-based chemotherapy for extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC), survival remains poor. In this study, we aimed to compare lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab and atezolizumab alone as maintenance therapies in patients with ES-SCLC without progression after induction therapy with atezolizumab, carboplatin, and etoposide.

Chunk 4: Between Nov 17, 2021, and Jan 11, 2024, 895 patients were screened for enrolment, of whom 660 (74%) were enrolled into the induction phase. Between May 24, 2022, and April 30, 2024, 483 (73%) of 660 patients entered the maintenance phase and were randomly assigned to lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab (n=242) or atezolizumab (n=241). At the data cutoff (July 29, 2024), IRF progression-free survival was longer in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group (stratified hazard ratio [HR] 0·54 [95% CI 0·43-0·67]; p<0·0001), as was overall survival (stratified HR 0·73 [0·57-0·95]; p=0·017). 92 (38%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and 53 (22%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group had grade 3-4 adverse events. The most common grade 3-4 events in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group were anaemia (20 [8%] of 242 patients), decreased neutrophil count (18 [7%] patients), and decreased platelet count (18 [7%] patients) and the most common events in the atezolizumab group were hyponatremia (five [2%] of 240 patients), dyspnoea (four [2%] patients), and pneumonia (four [2%] patients). Grade 5 adverse events occurred in 12 (5%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and six (3%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group. The incidence of myelosuppressive toxicities (eg, neutropenia and leukopenia) was higher in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05091567 titled 'A Phase III, Open-Label Study of Maintenance Lurbinectedin in Combination With Atezolizumab Compared With Atezolizumab in Participants With Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
ground_truth_answer: The exclusion criteria for the study protocol were not provided in the retrieved contexts. For detailed exclusion criteria, refer to the full study protocol available on ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT05091567).
source_chunks: Chunk 1: IMforte was a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial done at 96 hospitals and medical centres in 13 countries (Belgium, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Italy, Mexico, Poland, South Korea, Spain, Taiwan, Türkiye, the UK, and the USA). Eligible patients were aged 18 years or older with treatment-naive ES-SCLC. Patients received four 21-day cycles of induction treatment (atezolizumab, carboplatin, and etoposide). After completing induction treatment, eligible patients without disease progression were randomly assigned (1:1) using permuted blocks (Interactive Voice/Web Response System) to receive maintenance treatment intravenously every 3 weeks with lurbinectedin (3·2 mg/m2; with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor prophylaxis) plus atezolizumab (1200 mg) or atezolizumab (1200 mg). The two primary endpoints were independent review facility-assessed (IRF) progression-free survival and overall survival, measured from randomisation into the maintenance phase. Efficacy endpoints were assessed in the full analysis set, which included all patients who were randomly assigned to maintenance phase treatment, regardless of whether they received their assigned study treatment. Safety was assessed in all patients who received at least one dose of lurbinectedin or atezolizumab, and was analysed according to the treatment received. This study is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT05091567, and is closed for recruitment.

Chunk 3: Between Nov 17, 2021, and Jan 11, 2024, 895 patients were screened for enrolment, of whom 660 (74%) were enrolled into the induction phase. Between May 24, 2022, and April 30, 2024, 483 (73%) of 660 patients entered the maintenance phase and were randomly assigned to lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab (n=242) or atezolizumab (n=241). At the data cutoff (July 29, 2024), IRF progression-free survival was longer in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group (stratified hazard ratio [HR] 0·54 [95% CI 0·43-0·67]; p<0·0001), as was overall survival (stratified HR 0·73 [0·57-0·95]; p=0·017). 92 (38%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and 53 (22%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group had grade 3-4 adverse events. The most common grade 3-4 events in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group were anaemia (20 [8%] of 242 patients), decreased neutrophil count (18 [7%] patients), and decreased platelet count (18 [7%] patients) and the most common events in the atezolizumab group were hyponatremia (five [2%] of 240 patients), dyspnoea (four [2%] patients), and pneumonia (four [2%] patients). Grade 5 adverse events occurred in 12 (5%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and six (3%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group. The incidence of myelosuppressive toxicities (eg, neutropenia and leukopenia) was higher in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group.

Chunk 4: IRF progression-free survival and overall survival were longer in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group for patients with ES-SCLC, albeit with a higher incidence of adverse events. Lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab represents a novel therapeutic option for first-line maintenance treatment in this setting.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05091567 titled 'A Phase III, Open-Label Study of Maintenance Lurbinectedin in Combination With Atezolizumab Compared With Atezolizumab in Participants With Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoints of the study were independent review facility (IRF)-assessed progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS). PFS was measured as the time from randomization into the maintenance phase until disease progression or death, and OS was measured as the time from randomization until death. The predefined success threshold was achieving statistically significant results for both PFS and OS, with p-values less than 0.05.
source_chunks: The two primary endpoints were independent review facility-assessed (IRF) progression-free survival and overall survival, measured from randomisation into the maintenance phase.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05091567 titled 'A Phase III, Open-Label Study of Maintenance Lurbinectedin in Combination With Atezolizumab Compared With Atezolizumab in Participants With Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoints of IRF-assessed progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) were both met. For PFS, the stratified hazard ratio (HR) was 0.54 (95% CI: 0.43, 0.67) with a p-value of 0.0001. For OS, the stratified HR was 0.73 (95% CI: 0.57, 0.95) with a p-value of 0.0174. Both results were statistically significant, demonstrating a clinically meaningful benefit of lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab compared to atezolizumab alone in participants with extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05091567 titled 'A Phase III, Open-Label Study of Maintenance Lurbinectedin in Combination With Atezolizumab Compared With Atezolizumab in Participants With Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings for Clinical Study NCT05091567 include hazard ratios for progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS). The stratified hazard ratio for PFS was 0.54 (95% CI 0.43-0.67; p<0.0001), and for OS, it was 0.73 (95% CI 0.57-0.95; p=0.017). Adverse events were more frequent in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group, with 38% experiencing grade 3-4 events compared to 22% in the atezolizumab group. The most common grade 3-4 events in the combination group were anemia (8%), decreased neutrophil count (7%), and decreased platelet count (7%). In the atezolizumab group, the most common grade 3-4 events were hyponatremia (2%), dyspnoea (2%), and pneumonia (2%). Grade 5 adverse events occurred in 5% of the combination group and 3% of the atezolizumab group.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05091567 titled 'A Phase III, Open-Label Study of Maintenance Lurbinectedin in Combination With Atezolizumab Compared With Atezolizumab in Participants With Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis for Clinical Study NCT05091567 utilized stratified Cox proportional hazards models to determine hazard ratios (HR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) and stratified log-rank tests to compare survival curves. Kaplan-Meier estimates were employed to calculate median survival times. The analysis was conducted using the full analysis set, indicating an intention-to-treat (ITT) approach. No additional multiplicity corrections beyond stratification were mentioned.

ground_truth_answer: N/A. There is no mention of AI innovations such as AI-driven patient recruitment, AI-drug discovery, predictive modeling for outcomes, or image analysis for endpoints in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: IMforte was a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial done at 96 hospitals and medical centres in 13 countries (Belgium, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Italy, Mexico, Poland, South Korea, Spain, Taiwan, Türkiye, the UK, and the USA). Eligible patients were aged 18 years or older with treatment-naive ES-SCLC. Patients received four 21-day cycles of induction treatment (atezolizumab, carboplatin, and etoposide). After completing induction treatment, eligible patients without disease progression were randomly assigned (1:1) using permuted blocks (Interactive Voice/Web Response System) to receive maintenance treatment intravenously every 3 weeks with lurbinectedin (3·2 mg/m2; with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor prophylaxis) plus atezolizumab (1200 mg) or atezolizumab (1200 mg). The two primary endpoints were independent review facility-assessed (IRF) progression-free survival and overall survival, measured from randomisation into the maintenance phase. Efficacy endpoints were assessed in the full analysis set, which included all patients who were randomly assigned to maintenance phase treatment, regardless of whether they received their assigned study treatment. Safety was assessed in all patients who received at least one dose of lurbinectedin or atezolizumab, and was analysed according to the treatment received. This study is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT05091567, and is closed for recruitment.

Chunk 3: Between Nov 17, 2021, and Jan 11, 2024, 895 patients were screened for enrolment, of whom 660 (74%) were enrolled into the induction phase. Between May 24, 2022, and April 30, 2024, 483 (73%) of 660 patients entered the maintenance phase and were randomly assigned to lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab (n=242) or atezolizumab (n=241). At the data cutoff (July 29, 2024), IRF progression-free survival was longer in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group (stratified hazard ratio [HR] 0·54 [95% CI 0·43-0·67]; p<0·0001), as was overall survival (stratified HR 0·73 [0·57-0·95]; p=0·017). 92 (38%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and 53 (22%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group had grade 3-4 adverse events. The most common grade 3-4 events in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group were anaemia (20 [8%] of 242 patients), decreased neutrophil count (18 [7%] patients), and decreased platelet count (18 [7%] patients) and the most common events in the atezolizumab group were hyponatremia (five [2%] of 240 patients), dyspnoea (four [2%] patients), and pneumonia (four [2%] patients). Grade 5 adverse events occurred in 12 (5%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and six (3%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group. The incidence of myelosuppressive toxicities (eg, neutropenia and leukopenia) was higher in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group.

Chunk 4: IRF progression-free survival and overall survival were longer in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group for patients with ES-SCLC, albeit with a higher incidence of adverse events. Lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab represents a novel therapeutic option for first-line maintenance treatment in this setting.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: Participants were eligible if they were aged ≥18 years, had untreated, pathologically confirmed stage II, IIIA, or IIIB (N2) non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) as per the American Joint Committee on Cancer staging system (8th edition), were considered resectable after surgical consultation and investigator assessment, had an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 0 or 1, and provided a tumor sample for PD-L1 assessment at a central laboratory.
source_chunks: Participants were eligible for enrollment if they provided written, informed consent; were aged ≥18 years; had previously untreated, pathologically confirmed, stage II, IIIA, or IIIB (N2) NSCLC assessed per the American Joint Committee on Cancer staging system (8th edition14; details available in the Supplementary Methods and Table S1) considered resectable after surgical consultation and investigator assessment; had Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance-status score of 0 or 1 (on a 5-point scale, with 0 indicating no symptoms and higher scores indicating increasing disability15) within 10 days of randomization; and were able to provide a tumor sample for PD-L1 assessment at a central laboratory.

Participants were eligible for enrollment if they provided written, informed consent; were
aged ≥18 years; had previously untreated, pathologically confirmed, stage II, IIIA, or
IIIB (N2) NSCLC assessed per the American Joint Committee on Cancer staging system
(8th edition14; details available in the Supplementary Methods and Table S1) considered
resectable after surgical consultation and investigator assessment; had Eastern Cooperative
Oncology Group performance-status score of 0 or 1 (on a 5-point scale, with 0 indicating
no symptoms and higher scores indicating increasing disability15) within 10 days of

laboratory. Full eligibility criteria are available in Section 6 of the protocol, available with

Trial Design and Treatments
In this double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 study, randomization was performed
centrally using an interactive response system. Participants were stratified by stage (II vs
III), PD-L1 tumor proportion score (<50% vs ≥50% based on PD-L1 IHC 22C3 pharmDx
[Agilent Technologies]), histology (squamous vs nonsquamous), and geographic region
(east Asia vs not east Asia) and randomized (1:1) to receive pembrolizumab or placebo.
Neoadjuvant therapy comprised four cycles of pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo given
intravenously once every 3 weeks plus cisplatin and gemcitabine (squamous histology)
or cisplatin and pemetrexed (nonsquamous histology); 4 cycles of neoadjuvant therapy
were used in accordance with guideline recommendations for neoadjuvant therapy at the
time of study design in 2017. Surgery was to be performed per local standards no later
than 20 weeks after the first neoadjuvant therapy dose; radiotherapy was administered in
select circumstances. Adjuvant therapy was to be initiated no sooner than 4 weeks and
no later than 12 weeks after surgery and comprised pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo

placebo plus chemotherapy followed by surgery and adjuvant placebo (placebo group; n =

groups (Table 1) and generally representative of the broader lung cancer population (Table

global population, they accounted for 8 (10.3%) of the 78 participants enrolled in the United

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the study was event-free survival at 24 months, measured by the hazard ratio and 95% confidence interval. The predefined success threshold was a hazard ratio for progression, recurrence, or death of 0.58 with a 95% CI of 0.46 to 0.72 and a P value of <0.001.
source_chunks: Event-free survival at 24 months was 62.4% in the pembrolizumab group and 40.6% in the placebo group (hazard ratio for progression, recurrence, or death, 0.58; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.46 to 0.72; P<0.001).

Event-free survival was defined as the time from randomization to the first occurrence of local progression that precluded the planned surgery, unresectable tumor, progression or recurrence (according to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, version 1.1) by the investigator’s assessment, or death from any cause.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the study included major pathological response (≤10% viable tumor cells in resected primary tumor and lymph nodes) and pathological complete response (absence of residual invasive cancer in resected primary tumor and lymph nodes [ypT0/Tis ypN0]), both evaluated by a blinded, central pathologist. Safety was also a key secondary endpoint. Exploratory endpoints, such as quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes, were not mentioned in the provided contexts.

given intravenously once every 3 weeks for up to 13 additional cycles. Treatment was
continued until disease progression or recurrence, intolerable toxicity, investigator decision,
withdrawal of consent, or other reasons (summarized in the Supplementary Appendix).
Additional treatment information, including chemotherapy regimen and lymphadenectomy
details and circumstances in which radiotherapy was to be administered, is available in the
Supplementary Appendix.
Assessments and Endpoints
Pathological response following neoadjuvant therapy was assessed by examination of
hematoxylin-and-eosin-stained slides of resected lung tissue and lymph nodes. Definitions
of R0, R1, and R2 resection are outlined in the Supplementary Appendix. Computed
tomography (strongly preferred) or magnetic resonance imaging of the chest and abdomen
was performed during screening, throughout all treatment phases, and during follow-up
according to the schedule outlined in the Supplementary Appendix. While imaging was
performed after neoadjuvant therapy but before surgery, tumors were not formally restaged
before surgery. Participants were contacted for survival every 12 weeks. Adverse events and
laboratory abnormalities were assessed regularly throughout all treatment phases and for 30
days after discontinuation (up to 90 days for serious events in the absence of new anticancer
therapy) and graded according to the National Cancer Institute Common Terminology
Criteria for Adverse Events, version 4.03. Potentially immune-mediated adverse events and
infusion reactions were based on a list of preferred terms prepared by the sponsor and
considered regardless of attribution to treatment by the investigator. EGFR mutation and
ALK translocation status were tested locally per investigator discretion.
Dual primary endpoints were event-free survival (time from randomization to first
occurrence of local progression precluding planned surgery, unresectable tumor, progression
or recurrence per RECIST 1.1 by investigator assessment, or death from any cause)
and overall survival (time from randomization to death from any cause). Key secondary
endpoints included major pathological response (≤10% viable tumor cells in resected
primary tumor and lymph nodes) and pathological complete response (absence of residual
invasive cancer in resected primary tumor and lymph nodes [ypT0/Tis ypN0]) assessed by
blinded, central pathologist examination and safety.
Trial Oversight
A panel of academic advisors and sponsor employees designed the study. An external,
independent data and safety monitoring committee oversees the trial, assessing safety
regularly and efficacy at prespecified interim analyses. The protocol and all amendments
were approved by the appropriate ethics body for each participating center. All authors
vouch for data accuracy and completeness and study conduct in accordance with the
protocol, its amendments, and Good Clinical Practice guidelines. All authors attest they
participated in writing or reviewing and editing the manuscript. A medical writer employed
by the sponsor assisted with manuscript preparation.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint results for the clinical trial NCT03425643 were as follows: Event-free survival (EFS) at 24 months was 62.4% in the pembrolizumab group and 40.6% in the placebo group (hazard ratio for progression, recurrence, or death: 0.58; 95% CI, 0.46 to 0.72; P<0.001). Overall survival (OS) at 24 months was 80.9% in the pembrolizumab group and 77.6% in the placebo group (P=0.02). Major pathological response (MPR) occurred in 30.2% of participants in the pembrolizumab group and 11.0% in the placebo group (difference: 19.2 percentage points; 95% CI, 13.9 to 24.7; P<0.00001). Pathological complete response (PCR) occurred in 18.1% of participants in the pembrolizumab group and 4.0% in the placebo group (difference: 14.2 percentage points; 95% CI, 10.1 to 18.7; P<0.0001). The primary objective was met as at least one of the primary endpoints (event-free survival, major pathological response, and pathological complete response) was significantly improved in the pembrolizumab group.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: 'The study is considered positive if at least one of the primary endpoints is significantly improved in the pembrolizumab group.'

Chunk 4: 'Major pathological response occurred in 120 (30.2%; 95% CI, 25.7–35.0) participants in the pembrolizumab group and 44 (11.0%; 95% CI, 8.1–14.5) participants in the placebo group (difference, 19.2; 95% CI, 13.9–24.7; P<0.00001). Pathological complete response occurred in 72 (18.1%; 95% CI, 14.5–22.3) participants in the pembrolizumab group and 16 (4.0%; 95% CI, 2.3–6.4) participants in the placebo group (difference, 14.2; 95% CI, 10.1–18.7; P<0.0001).'

Chunk 5: 'Event-free survival at 24 months was 62.4% in the pembrolizumab group and 40.6% in the placebo group (hazard ratio for progression, recurrence, or death, 0.58; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.46 to 0.72; P<0.001). The estimated 24-month overall survival was 80.9% in the pembrolizumab group and 77.6% in the placebo group (P = 0.02, which did not meet the significance criterion). A major pathological response occurred in 30.2% of the participants in the pembrolizumab group and in 11.0% of those in the placebo group (difference, 19.2 percentage points; 95% CI, 13.9 to 24.7; P<0.0001; threshold, P = 0.0001), and a pathological complete response occurred in 18.1% and 4.0%, respectively (difference, 14.2 percentage points; 95% CI, 10.1 to 18.7; P<0.0001; threshold, P = 0.0001).'

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings for Clinical Study NCT03425643 include safety assessments, with the pembrolizumab group showing higher rates of treatment-related adverse events compared to the placebo group. Specifically, 96.7% of participants in the pembrolizumab group experienced any treatment-related adverse event, compared to 95.0% in the placebo group. Grade 3–5 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 44.9% of the pembrolizumab group versus 37.3% in the placebo group. Serious treatment-related adverse events were reported in 17.7% of the pembrolizumab group and 14.3% of the placebo group. Treatment-related adverse events leading to death occurred in 1.0% of the pembrolizumab group and 0.8% of the placebo group. Adverse events leading to discontinuation of all trial treatment were reported in 12.6% of the pembrolizumab group and 5.3% of the placebo group. The study employed stratified Cox regression models and the stratified log-rank test for between-group comparisons, with adjustments for multiple comparisons using the Maurer and Bretz method to control the family-wise type I error rate.
source_chunks: Any treatment-related adverse event: 383 (96.7) vs 379 (95.0)
Grade 3–5 treatment-related adverse event: 178 (44.9) vs 149 (37.3)
Serious treatment-related adverse event: 70 (17.7) vs 57 (14.3)
Treatment-related adverse event that led to death: 4 (1.0) vs 3 (0.8)
Treatment-related adverse event that led to discontinuation of all trial treatment: 50 (12.6) vs 21 (5.3)

The magnitude of the treatment differences (i.e., hazard ratios and associated 95% confidence intervals [CIs]) was calculated using a stratified Cox regression model with treatment as a covariate and Efron’s method of handling ties; between-group differences were assessed using the stratified log-rank test.

The graphical method of Maurer and Bretz was used to strictly control the family-wise type I error rate at one-sided α=0.025 across the event-free survival, overall survival, major pathological response, and pathological complete response hypotheses among the interim and final analyses.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis used Kaplan-Meier estimates for event-free survival and overall survival, stratified Cox regression models with Efron’s method for handling ties, and the stratified log-rank test for between-group differences. The intention-to-treat population was used for efficacy assessments, while the as-treated population was used for safety. Multiplicity corrections were applied using the Maurer and Bretz graphical method and the Lan-DeMets O’Brien-Fleming spending function. The Miettinen and Nurminen method with sample size weighting was used for response rate comparisons.
source_chunks: Efficacy was assessed in the intention-to-treat population (all randomized participants). [...] Event-free survival and overall survival were estimated using the Kaplan-Meier method. [...] The magnitude of the treatment differences [...] was calculated using a stratified Cox regression model with treatment as a covariate and Efron’s method of handling ties; between-group differences were assessed using the stratified log-rank test.

The graphical method of Maurer and Bretz was used to strictly control the family-wise type I error rate at one-sided α=0.025 [...] The Lan-DeMets O’Brien-Fleming spending function will be used to control type I error for event-free survival and overall survival in the interim and final analyses.

Between-group comparisons of the percentage of participants with major pathological response and the percentage with pathological complete response were performed using the stratified Miettinen and Nurminen method with strata weighting by sample size.

Chunk 4: Participants
Participants were eligible for enrollment if they provided written, informed consent; were
aged ≥18 years; had previously untreated, pathologically confirmed, stage II, IIIA, or
IIIB (N2) NSCLC assessed per the American Joint Committee on Cancer staging system
(8th edition14; details available in the Supplementary Methods and Table S1) considered
resectable after surgical consultation and investigator assessment; had Eastern Cooperative
Oncology Group performance-status score of 0 or 1 (on a 5-point scale, with 0 indicating
no symptoms and higher scores indicating increasing disability15) within 10 days of
randomization; and were able to provide a tumor sample for PD-L1 assessment at a central
laboratory. Full eligibility criteria are available in Section 6 of the protocol, available with
the full text of this article at NEJM.org.
Trial Design and Treatments
In this double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 study, randomization was performed
centrally using an interactive response system. Participants were stratified by stage (II vs
III), PD-L1 tumor proportion score (<50% vs ≥50% based on PD-L1 IHC 22C3 pharmDx
[Agilent Technologies]), histology (squamous vs nonsquamous), and geographic region
(east Asia vs not east Asia) and randomized (1:1) to receive pembrolizumab or placebo.
Neoadjuvant therapy comprised four cycles of pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo given
intravenously once every 3 weeks plus cisplatin and gemcitabine (squamous histology)
or cisplatin and pemetrexed (nonsquamous histology); 4 cycles of neoadjuvant therapy
were used in accordance with guideline recommendations for neoadjuvant therapy at the
time of study design in 2017. Surgery was to be performed per local standards no later
than 20 weeks after the first neoadjuvant therapy dose; radiotherapy was administered in
select circumstances. Adjuvant therapy was to be initiated no sooner than 4 weeks and
no later than 12 weeks after surgery and comprised pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo

Chunk 5: We conducted a randomized, double-blind, phase 3 trial to evaluate perioperative pembrolizumab in patients with early-stage NSCLC. Participants with resectable stage II, IIIA, or IIIB (N2 stage) NSCLC were assigned in a 1:1 ratio to receive neoadjuvant pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo once every 3 weeks, each of which was given with cisplatin-based chemotherapy for 4 cycles, followed by surgery and adjuvant pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo once every 3 weeks for up to 13 cycles. The dual primary end points were event-free survival (the time from randomization to the first occurrence of local progression that precluded the planned surgery, unresectable tumor, progression or recurrence, or death) and overall survival. Secondary end points included major pathological response, pathological complete response, and safety.

ground_truth_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial included patients with limited stage small cell lung cancer (LS-SCLC), stage Tx-4, N0-3, M0, and an ECOG performance status of 0-2. The exact age range and required biomarkers were not mentioned in these contexts.

Chunk 2: ClinicalTrials.gov NCT03811002; https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03811002.

Chunk 3: The purpose of this study is to formally create an easily implemented tool kit of resources for investigators of oncology clinical trials within the NCTN, specifically the NRG Oncology cooperative group, in order to optimize patient accrual.

Chunk 4: NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 has seen robust enrollment, currently 127% of the projected accrual. Importantly, many of the tool kit elements are already being used in ongoing NRG Oncology trials, with 56% of active NRG trials using at least one element of the PI tool kit and all in-development trials offered the resource. This underscores the feasibility and potential benefits of deploying the PI tool kit across all NRG Oncology trials moving forward.

Chunk 5: NRG Oncology sought to formally develop a tool kit of resources to use at specific time points during the lifetime of NRG Oncology clinical trials. The tools are clearly described and involve the facilitation of engagement of the study principal investigator with the scientific and patient advocate community during the planning, activation, and accrual periods. Social media tools are also leveraged to enhance such engagement. The principal investigator (PI) tool kit was created in 2019 and thereafter piloted with the NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 phase II or III trial in small-cell lung cancer. The PI tool kit was developed by the NRG Oncology Protocol Operations Management committee and was tested with the NRG/Alliance LU005 randomized trial within the NCTN.

Chunk 6: Accrual to oncology clinical trials remains a challenge, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic. For late phase clinical trials funded by the National Cancer Institute, the development of these research protocols is a resource-intensive process; however, mechanisms to optimize patient accrual after trial activation are underdeveloped across the National Clinical Trial Network (NCTN). Low patient accrual can lead to the premature closure of clinical trials and can ultimately delay the availability of new, potentially life-saving therapies in oncology.

source_chunks: Chunk 1: ClinicalTrials.gov NCT03811002; https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03811002.

Chunk 2: NRG Oncology sought to formally develop a tool kit of resources to use at specific time points during the lifetime of NRG Oncology clinical trials. The tools are clearly described and involve the facilitation of engagement of the study principal investigator with the scientific and patient advocate community during the planning, activation, and accrual periods. Social media tools are also leveraged to enhance such engagement. The principal investigator (PI) tool kit was created in 2019 and thereafter piloted with the NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 phase II or III trial in small-cell lung cancer. The PI tool kit was developed by the NRG Oncology Protocol Operations Management committee and was tested with the NRG/Alliance LU005 randomized trial within the NCTN.

Chunk 3: Accrual to oncology clinical trials remains a challenge, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic. For late phase clinical trials funded by the National Cancer Institute, the development of these research protocols is a resource-intensive process; however, mechanisms to optimize patient accrual after trial activation are underdeveloped across the National Clinical Trial Network (NCTN). Low patient accrual can lead to the premature closure of clinical trials and can ultimately delay the availability of new, potentially life-saving therapies in oncology.

Chunk 4: NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 has seen robust enrollment, currently 127% of the projected accrual. Importantly, many of the tool kit elements are already being used in ongoing NRG Oncology trials, with 56% of active NRG trials using at least one element of the PI tool kit and all in-development trials offered the resource. This underscores the feasibility and potential benefits of deploying the PI tool kit across all NRG Oncology trials moving forward.

Chunk 5: The purpose of this study is to formally create an easily implemented tool kit of resources for investigators of oncology clinical trials within the NCTN, specifically the NRG Oncology cooperative group, in order to optimize patient accrual.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03811002 titled 'Testing the Addition of a New Immunotherapy Drug, Atezolizumab (MPDL3280A), to the Usual Chemoradiation (CRT) Therapy Treatment for Limited Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer (LS-SCLC)': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the study was overall survival (OS), measured by the hazard ratio (HR) of 0.71. The predefined success threshold was a 1-sided alpha of 0.025 with 85% power.
source_chunks: The primary endpoint was overall survival (OS).
It was designed to detect an OS improvement with a hazard ratio (HR) of 0.71, at 1-sided alpha of 0.025 and 85% power.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03811002 titled 'Testing the Addition of a New Immunotherapy Drug, Atezolizumab (MPDL3280A), to the Usual Chemoradiation (CRT) Therapy Treatment for Limited Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer (LS-SCLC)': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint for the clinical trial NCT03811002 was overall survival (OS). The results showed that the median OS was 39.5 months (95% CI: 27.5 - Not reached) for the control arm (cCRT) and 33.1 months (95% CI: 27.8 - 43.9) for the atezo + cCRT arm. The hazard ratio (HR) was 1.11 (95% CI: 0.85 - 1.45). The primary objective of demonstrating an improvement in OS with a hazard ratio of 0.71 was not met, as the observed HR was 1.11, indicating no significant benefit from adding atezolizumab. The p-value was not mentioned in the provided contexts.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03811002 titled 'Testing the Addition of a New Immunotherapy Drug, Atezolizumab (MPDL3280A), to the Usual Chemoradiation (CRT) Therapy Treatment for Limited Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer (LS-SCLC)': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings for Clinical Study NCT03811002 include: progression-free survival (PFS) with a median of 11.5 months (95% CI: 10.7-14.3) for the control arm and 11.4 months (95% CI: 9.8-14.1) for the experimental arm (HR=0.95, 95% CI: 0.75-1.21). Local failure at 24 months was 14.4% (95% CI: 10.1-19.5) for the control arm and 13.1% (95% CI: 9.0-18.3) for the experimental arm. Distant-metastasis free survival (DMFS) had a median of 13.2 months (95% CI: 11.9-15.4) for the control arm and 13.1 months (95% CI: 11.1-15.4) for the experimental arm (HR=1.00, 95% CI: 0.80-1.25). Objective response rate (ORR) is not mentioned in these contexts. No significant adjustments to the secondary endpoints were noted.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03811002 titled 'Testing the Addition of a New Immunotherapy Drug, Atezolizumab (MPDL3280A), to the Usual Chemoradiation (CRT) Therapy Treatment for Limited Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer (LS-SCLC)': Did this clinical study incorporate any AI innovations, such as AI-driven patient recruitment, Ai-drug discovery, predictive modeling for outcomes, or image analysis for endpoints
ground_truth_answer: N/A. The provided contexts do not mention the use of AI innovations such as AI-driven patient recruitment, AI-drug discovery, predictive modeling for outcomes, or image analysis for endpoints in Clinical Study NCT03811002.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: ClinicalTrials.gov NCT03811002; https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03811002.

Chunk 2: NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 has seen robust enrollment, currently 127% of the projected accrual. Importantly, many of the tool kit elements are already being used in ongoing NRG Oncology trials, with 56% of active NRG trials using at least one element of the PI tool kit and all in-development trials offered the resource. This underscores the feasibility and potential benefits of deploying the PI tool kit across all NRG Oncology trials moving forward.

Chunk 3: While clinical trial accrual can be challenging, the PI tool kit has been shown to augment accrual in a low-cost and easily implementable fashion. It could be widely and consistently deployed across the NCTN to improve accrual in oncology clinical trials.

Chunk 4: NRG Oncology sought to formally develop a tool kit of resources to use at specific time points during the lifetime of NRG Oncology clinical trials. The tools are clearly described and involve the facilitation of engagement of the study principal investigator with the scientific and patient advocate community during the planning, activation, and accrual periods. Social media tools are also leveraged to enhance such engagement. The principal investigator (PI) tool kit was created in 2019 and thereafter piloted with the NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 phase II or III trial in small-cell lung cancer. The PI tool kit was developed by the NRG Oncology Protocol Operations Management committee and was tested with the NRG/Alliance LU005 randomized trial within the NCTN.

Chunk 5: The purpose of this study is to formally create an easily implemented tool kit of resources for investigators of oncology clinical trials within the NCTN, specifically the NRG Oncology cooperative group, in order to optimize patient accrual.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04676412 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) [MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007] - China Extension Study': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: Participants in the clinical trial NCT04676412 were required to have PD-L1-positive, treatment-naïve non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with locally advanced or metastatic disease. The inclusion criteria specified an age range of 34 to 85 years, an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 0 or 1, and a PD-L1 Tumor Proportion Score (TPS) of at least 1%. Exclusion criteria included active autoimmune diseases requiring systemic treatment, history of non-infectious pneumonitis, clinically significant hemoptysis, untreated or unstable central nervous system metastases, and certain cardiovascular conditions.
source_chunks: Patients were ineligible if they had clinically important hemoptysis (defined as 0.5 teaspoon of bright red blood) or tumor bleeding within 2 weeks before study treatment; either known central nervous system metastases or carcinomatous meningitis or both, unless previously treated and radiologically stable (i.e., no evidence of progression for 4 weeks per repeat imaging performed during screening), clinically stable, and with no steroid treatment required within 14 days before study treatment; active autoimmune disease that required systemic treatment in the previous 2 years; history of non-infectious pneumonitis that required steroids or current pneumonitis/interstitial lung disease; diagnosis of immunodeficiency or immunosuppressive therapy within 7 days before study treatment; history of a gastrointestinal condition/procedure that could affect absorption of oral lenvatinib; or clinically relevant cardiovascular impairment (e.g., congestive heart failure classified as greater than New York Heart Association class II, unstable angina, myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or cardiac arrhythmia associated with hemodynamic instability) within 12 months before study treatment.

Archival tumor tissue samples or newly obtained core or excisional biopsy samples of a tumor lesion not previously irradiated were centrally evaluated for PD-L1 expression during screening using PD-L1 IHC 22C3 pharmDx (Agilent Technologies, Carpinteria, CA).

question: For Clinical Study NCT04676412 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) [MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007] - China Extension Study': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
ground_truth_answer: Exclusion criteria for the study included: history of gastrointestinal conditions affecting lenvatinib absorption; clinically relevant cardiovascular impairment (e.g., congestive heart failure, unstable angina, myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or arrhythmia with hemodynamic instability) within 12 months; prior anti-PD-1/anti-PD-L1 therapy or lenvatinib treatment; recent radiotherapy (within 14 days or >30 Gy within 6 months); radiographic evidence of major blood vessel invasion or intratumoral cavitation; clinically important hemoptysis (defined as 0.5 teaspoon of bright red blood) or tumor bleeding within 2 weeks before study treatment; central nervous system metastases or carcinomatous meningitis unless previously treated and stable; active autoimmune disease requiring systemic treatment within the previous 2 years; history of non-infectious pneumonitis requiring steroids or current pneumonitis/interstitial lung disease; immunodeficiency or immunosuppressive therapy within 7 days of treatment; uncontrolled blood pressure (systolic blood pressure ≥150/90 mmHg without change in antihypertensive medication within 1 week before randomization); and brain metastases or liver metastases not meeting specific stability criteria.
source_chunks: history of a gastrointestinal condition/procedure that could affect absorption of orallenvatinib; or clinically relevant cardiovascular impairment (e.g., congestive heart failure classified as greater than New York Heart Association class II, unstable angina, myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or cardiac arrhythmia associated with hemodynamic instability) within 12 months before study treatment.

Additional exclusion criteria included prior anti‒PD-1/anti‒PD-L1/anti‒PD-L2 therapy or any treatment directed at another stimulatory or co-inhibitory T-cell receptor, prior lenvatinib treatment as monotherapy or in combination with anti‒PD-1 agents, or any radiotherapy within 14 days or lung radiotherapy of more than 30 Gy within 6 months before initiation of study treatment.

Patients were ineligible if there was radiographic evidence of invasion or infiltration of a major blood vessel. Despite this, fatal bleeding events occurred in a few patients who were retrospectively found to have major blood vessel encasement at enrollment, and thus the protocol was amended to specify exclusion of encasement of a major blood vessel or intratumoral cavitation.

Patients were ineligible if they had clinically important hemoptysis (defined as 0.5 teaspoon of bright red blood) or tumor bleeding within 2 weeks before study treatment; either known central nervous system metastases or carcinomatous meningitis or both, unless previously treated and radio-logically stable (i.e., no evidence of progression for 4 weeks per repeat imaging during screening), clinically stable, and with no steroid treatment required within 14 days before study treatment; active autoimmune disease that required systemic treatment in the previous 2 years; history of non-infectious pneumonitis that required steroids or current pneumonitis/interstitial lung disease; diagnosis of immunodeficiency or immunosuppressive therapy within 7 days before study treatment.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04676412 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) [MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007] - China Extension Study': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoints of the study were Overall Survival (OS) and Progression-Free Survival (PFS). OS was measured as the time from randomization to death from any cause, and PFS was measured as the time from randomization to the first documented assessment of progressive disease (PD). The predefined success thresholds were a hazard ratio (HR) less than 0.9 for OS and an HR less than 1.0 for PFS.
source_chunks: The study had two primary end points: overall survival (OS), defined as time from randomization to death because of any cause, and progression-free survival (PFS), defined as time from randomization to the first documented assessment of PD (per RECIST version 1.1)
If the OS HR was less than 0.9, then the futility criterion was not met.
If the PFS HR was less than 1, representing no PFS harm, then the futility criterion was not met.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04676412 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) [MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007] - China Extension Study': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the study included Objective Response Rate (ORR) and safety. An exploratory endpoint was the duration of response, defined as the time from the earliest documented response to the first occurrence of progressive disease or death. Quality-of-life scores and biomarker changes were not mentioned as endpoints in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Secondary end points were ORR, defined as a confirmed complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) per RECIST version 1.1 by BICR, and safety. Duration of response, defined as time from earliest documented response to the first of either PD or death because of any cause, was an exploratory end point.
Secondary end points were ORR, defined as a confirmed complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) per RECIST version 1.1 by BICR, and safety.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04676412 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) [MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007] - China Extension Study': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoints for the China Extension Study were overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS). The results suggested worse OS outcomes with lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab compared to placebo plus pembrolizumab, with a wide confidence interval likely due to the smaller sample size and low proportion of events. The primary objective was not met as the study did not demonstrate significant efficacy in OS, PFS, or ORR. Point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met: N/A (not mentioned in these contexts).
source_chunks: Chunk 3: Nevertheless, it was recently reported that at the final study analysis, both LEAP-006 and LEAP-008 did not meet their primary and secondary efficacy end points, including OS, PFS, and ORR. The full publication of data from these studies could provide insight into which patient population may have benefited the most from this treatment combination. In conclusion, although there was evidence of clinical benefit based on PFS and ORR findings with lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab in this randomized, double-blind,

Chunk 3: Similar to the global study, results from the China study suggested worse OS outcomes with lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab versus placebo plus pembrolizumab, although the CI was wide, likely owing to the smaller sample size and low proportion of events.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04676412 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) [MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007] - China Extension Study': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer:
The secondary endpoint findings for the clinical trial NCT04676412 included a hazard ratio (HR) for progression-free survival (PFS) of 0.78 (95% CI, 0.64-0.95) with a nominal p-value of 0.00624. The HR for overall survival (OS) was 1.10, though the 95% confidence interval was not provided in the available context. The objective response rate (ORR) was evaluated as a secondary endpoint but was only formally tested at the first interim analysis. 

The futility analysis for OS had a boundary of HR ≤ 0.9, and for PFS, the boundary was HR < 1.0. The alpha initially assigned to ORR (one-sided alpha of 0.001) was reallocated to the primary endpoints of PFS and OS. No additional significant adjustments were mentioned in the context provided.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04676412 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) [MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007] - China Extension Study': What were the most common adverse events (grade 3+), their incidence rates by arm, and any serious events leading to discontinuation
ground_truth_answer: The most common adverse events (grade 3+) in the lenvatinib + pembrolizumab arm were hypertension (17.2%), proteinuria (6.1%), and diarrhea (3.2%). In the placebo + pembrolizumab arm, the most common were hypertension (2.6%), proteinuria (1.0%), and diarrhea (0.6%). Serious events leading to discontinuation occurred in 29.1% of the lenvatinib + pembrolizumab group and 11.2% of the placebo + pembrolizumab group.
