question: For Clinical Study NCT04676412 titled 'Efficacy and Safety Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With or Without Lenvatinib (MK-7902/E7080) in Adults With Programmed Cell Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1)-Positive Treatment-naïve Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) [MK-7902-007/E7080-G000-314/LEAP-007] - China Extension Study': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis for Clinical Study NCT04676412 utilized Kaplan-Meier estimates to assess overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS). Stratified log-rank tests were employed to evaluate differences between treatment groups. Additionally, stratified Cox proportional hazard models with Efron’s method for handling ties were used to determine the magnitude of treatment differences. The intention-to-treat (ITT) population was analyzed for efficacy, while the as-treated population was considered for safety assessments. Multiplicity corrections involved reallocating alpha levels to the primary endpoints (OS and PFS) and implementing prespecified futility boundaries, such as a futility criterion for OS with a hazard ratio threshold of 0.9 and for PFS with a threshold of 1.0. These methods ensured a robust evaluation of the treatment effects while controlling for multiple comparisons and potential biases.
source_chunks: Efficacy analyses were performed in all randomized patients according to assigned treatment (intention-to-treat population). Safety analyses were performed in all randomized patients who received at least one dose of the study treatment according to treatment received (as-treated population). OS and PFS were estimated using the nonparametric Kaplan-Meier method. Between-group treatment differences in OS and PFS were assessed using the stratified log-rank test; all p-values are one-sided. The magnitude of the treatment difference was calculated using a stratified Cox proportional hazard model with Efron’s method of tie handling.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05089734 titled 'Study of Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) Versus Docetaxel in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in the clinical trial NCT05089734 included patients aged between 31 and 84 years with stage IV disease. Specific biomarkers required for inclusion were not mentioned in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Between November 17, 2021, and May 30, 2023, 603 patients with stage IV disease at study entry from 177 sites in 20 countries were randomly assigned to receive SG (n = 299) or docetaxel (n = 304; Fig 1).
Patients in the SG and docetaxel groups had a median age of 65 years (range, 31-84) and 64 years (range, 32-83), respectively.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05089734 titled 'Study of Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) Versus Docetaxel in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
ground_truth_answer: The trial design for NCT05089734 (EVOKE-01) was a randomized, open-label, controlled, parallel study. The sample size per arm was 299 participants allocated to Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) and 304 participants allocated to docetaxel. The trial was not blinded as it was open-label.

Chunk 5: Patients were randomly assigned 1:1 (stratified by histology, best response to last anti-PD-(L)1-containing regimen, and AGA treatment received or not) to SG (one 10 mg/kg intravenous infusion on days 1 and 8) or docetaxel (one 75 mg/m2 intravenous infusion on day 1) in 21-day cycles. Primary end point was overall survival (OS). Key secondary end points were investigator-assessed progression-free survival (PFS), objective response rate, patient-reported symptom assessment, and safety.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05089734 titled 'Study of Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) Versus Docetaxel in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the study was overall survival (OS), measured using the Kaplan-Meier method with 95% confidence intervals determined according to the Brookmeyer-Crowley method. The predefined success threshold for statistical significance was a p-value of ≤0.0223.
source_chunks: The primary endpoint was overall survival (OS).
The boundary for statistical significance of the primary endpoint was ≤.0223.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05089734 titled 'Study of Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) Versus Docetaxel in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the clinical trial NCT05089734 included investigator-assessed progression-free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DoR), disease control rate (DCR), and safety. Quality-of-life scores were evaluated using the NSCLC-SAQ questionnaire, specifically assessing time to deterioration in the shortness-of-breath domain and total score. No additional exploratory endpoints beyond these were mentioned in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Secondary efficacy endpoints were investigator-assessed progression-free survival (PFS), ORR (percentage of patients with confirmed CR or PR), DoR, disease control rate (DCR; percentage of patients with confirmed SD, CR, or PR) as determined by RECIST version 1.1, and safety. Additional secondary end points included symptoms assessed by patient-reported time to deterioration (TTD) in the NSCLC Symptom Assessment Questionnaire (SAQ) shortness-of-breath domain (decline from baseline in one point on a five-point scale) and total score (decline from baseline in two points on a 20-point scale).

question: For Clinical Study NCT05089734 titled 'Study of Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) Versus Docetaxel in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint for the clinical trial NCT05089734 was overall survival (OS). The results showed a median OS of 11.1 months for Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) and 9.8 months for docetaxel. The hazard ratio (HR) was 0.84 with a 95% confidence interval (CI) of 0.68 to 1.04. The one-sided p-value was .0534, which did not meet the significance boundary of .0223, indicating the primary objective was not achieved.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05089734 titled 'Study of Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) Versus Docetaxel in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints for Clinical Study NCT05089734 included progression-free survival (PFS), overall response rate (ORR), duration of response (DoR), disease control rate (DCR), and time to deterioration (TTD) in the NSCLC-SAQ. PFS and TTD were analyzed using the Kaplan-Meier method with stratified log-rank tests, and response rates were compared using the Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel method. Safety assessments included treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAEs), with 99.7% of patients in the SG group and 97.9% in the docetaxel group experiencing TEAEs. Significant adjustments included a Lan-DeMets alpha spending function approximating an O'Brien-Fleming approach for interim analysis. Key findings for secondary endpoints were as follows: TTD in the NSCLC-SAQ shortness-of-breath domain was longer for SG versus docetaxel (median 2.8 vs. 2.1 months; HR, 0.75 [95% CI, 0.61 to 0.91]). Similarly, TTD in the total score was longer for SG versus docetaxel (median 3.1 vs. 2.7 months; HR, 0.78 [95% CI, 0.64 to 0.95]).
source_chunks: Secondary efficacy end points were investigator-assessed progression-free survival (PFS), ORR (percentage of patients with confirmed CR or PR), DoR, disease control rate (DCR; percentage of patients with confirmed SD, CR, or PR) as determined by RECIST version 1.1, and safety.

OS, PFS, and TTD were analyzed using the Kaplan-Meier method, with 95% CIs determined according to the Brookmeyer-Crowley method. Between-treatment group differences were assessed by stratified log-rank test. HRs and associated 95% CIs were estimated by a stratified Cox proportional hazards model. Response rates were compared between groups by the stratified Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel method.

TEAEs were summarized in each group using descriptive statistics for treatment-emergent AEs (TEAEs; defined as any AEs that began or worsened on/after study drug administration through 30 days after last dose of study drug) unless otherwise specified.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05089734 titled 'Study of Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) Versus Docetaxel in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)': What were the most common adverse events (grade 3+), their incidence rates by arm, and any serious events leading to discontinuation
ground_truth_answer: The most common adverse events (grade 3+) in the SG arm were fatigue (12.5%), diarrhea (10.5%), and neutropenia (7.8%), while in the docetaxel arm, the most common were fatigue (9.7%), neutropenia (9.4%), and dyspnea (4.5%). Serious events leading to discontinuation included febrile neutropenia, sepsis, and others, affecting 9.8% in SG and 16.7% in docetaxel.
source_chunks: Chunk 2: The proportion of patients with TEAEs leading to study treatment discontinuation was lower with SG (n = 29, 9.8%) than docetaxel (n = 48, 16.7%).

question: For Clinical Study NCT05089734 titled 'Study of Sacituzumab Govitecan (SG) Versus Docetaxel in Participants With Advanced or Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis used the Kaplan-Meier method with Brookmeyer-Crowley 95% CIs, a stratified log-rank test, and a stratified Cox proportional hazards model for HRs. Multiplicity was addressed using a Lan-DeMets alpha spending function approximating an O’Brien-Fleming approach. The ITT population was used for efficacy assessments.
source_chunks: OS, PFS, and TTD were analyzed using the Kaplan-Meier method, with 95% CIs determined according to the Brookmeyer-Crowley method. Between-treatment group differences were assessed by stratified log-rank test. HRs and associated 95% CIs were estimated by a stratified Cox proportional hazards model.
A Lan-DeMets alpha spending function that approximates an O’Brien-Fleming approach was used to account for multiplicity introduced by including an interim analysis for OS.
Efficacy end points were evaluated in the ITT population.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04256421 titled 'A Study of Atezolizumab Plus Carboplatin and Etoposide With or Without Tiragolumab in Patients With Untreated Extensive-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in the clinical trial NCT04256421 included patients with untreated extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC). Specific criteria mentioned were that patients with treated or untreated brain metastases were permitted, provided the metastases were asymptomatic and measurable disease was present outside the central nervous system. The exact age range, disease stage beyond ES-SCLC, and required biomarkers were not explicitly mentioned in the provided contexts.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04256421 titled 'A Study of Atezolizumab Plus Carboplatin and Etoposide With or Without Tiragolumab in Patients With Untreated Extensive-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
ground_truth_answer: The exclusion criteria for the study protocol include patients who did not meet eligibility criteria (n = 162), patient decision (n = 6), withdrawal by physician (n = 0), died (n = 0), and other reasons (n = 1). Additionally, patients with brain metastases at baseline were excluded from the Per Protocol Set (PAS). Prior treatments, comorbidities, or specific lab value thresholds that disqualified patients are not mentioned in these contexts.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04256421 titled 'A Study of Atezolizumab Plus Carboplatin and Etoposide With or Without Tiragolumab in Patients With Untreated Extensive-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the study was progression-free survival (PFS) in the primary analysis set (PAS), defined as the time from random assignment to the first occurrence of progressive disease (PD) or death from any cause. The study was powered to detect a target hazard ratio (HR) of 0.56 for PFS at a two-sided significance level of 0.001.
source_chunks: The primary analysis of the primary efficacy endpoint, PFS in the PAS, was planned at the time of the OS efficacy interim analysis when approximately 202 deaths had been observed in the PAS.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04256421 titled 'A Study of Atezolizumab Plus Carboplatin and Etoposide With or Without Tiragolumab in Patients With Untreated Extensive-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed included progression-free survival (PFS), overall survival (OS), confirmed objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), PFS rate at 6 and 12 months, and OS rate at 12 and 24 months. Exploratory endpoints included biomarker analyses, such as the TIGIT pathway, and adverse events (AEs) evaluation. Quality-of-life scores were not mentioned in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Secondary end points were PFS and OS in all randomly assigned patients, irrespective of brain metastases status at baseline (full analysis set [FAS]); confirmed objective response rate (ORR), defined as the proportion of patients with a complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) on two consecutive occasions ≥4 weeks apart; duration of response (DOR) for patients with confirmed ORR; PFS rate at 6 and 12 months; and OS rate at 12 and 24 months. One efficacy interim and one final analysis of OS was planned.

Adverse events (AEs) were evaluated in all randomly assigned patients who received at least one dose of study treatment, with severity assessed according to National Cancer Institute Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events version 5.0.

Pre- and on-treatment samples of tumor tissue, serum, plasma, and blood were collected as part of the exploratory biomarker objective to identify potential biomarkers of treatment response, including the TIGIT pathway, prognosis, acquired resistance, pharmacodynamics, and safety.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04256421 titled 'A Study of Atezolizumab Plus Carboplatin and Etoposide With or Without Tiragolumab in Patients With Untreated Extensive-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer:
The primary endpoint results for Clinical Study NCT04256421 were evaluated in the primary analysis set (PAS) and full analysis set (FAS). The primary endpoints were investigator-assessed progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) in the PAS, which included patients without a history or presence of brain metastases at baseline.

For PFS in the PAS:
- Median PFS: 5.4 months in the tiragolumab arm and 5.6 months in the control arm.
- Hazard Ratio (HR): 1.11 (stratified).
- P-value: 0.3504.
- Conclusion: The result did not reach statistical significance.

For OS in the PAS:
- Median OS: 13.1 months in both the tiragolumab and control arms.
- Hazard Ratio (HR): 1.14 (stratified).
- P-value: 0.2859.
- Conclusion: The result did not reach statistical significance.

The primary objective was not met for either PFS or OS in the PAS. The results for PFS and OS in the FAS were consistent with those in the PAS.

Chunk 3: Patients received tiragolumab 600 mg/placebo, plus atezolizumab 1,200 mg and CE (four cycles), then maintenance tiragolumab/placebo plus atezolizumab. Primary end points were investigator-assessed PFS and OS in patients without history/presence of brain metastases (primary analysis set [PAS]). Additional end points included PFS and OS in all patients regardless of brain metastases status (full analysis set [FAS]), response, and safety.

Chunk 4: Four hundred ninety patients were randomly assigned (FAS): 243 to tiragolumab arm and 247 to control arm. At the cutoff date (February 6, 2022; median duration of follow-up, 14.3 months [PAS] and 13.9 months [FAS]), final analysis of PFS in the PAS (n = 397) did not reach statistical significance (stratified hazard ratio [HR], 1.11; P = .3504; median, 5.4 months tiragolumab v 5.6 months control). At the cutoff date (September 6, 2022; median duration of follow-up, 21.2 months [FAS]), median OS in the PAS at final OS analysis was 13.1 months in both arms (stratified HR, 1.14; P = .2859). Median PFS and OS in the FAS were consistent with the PAS. The proportion of patients with immune-mediated adverse events (AEs) in the tiragolumab and control arms was 54.4% and 49.2%, respectively (grade 3/4: 7.9% and 7.7%). AEs leading to treatment withdrawal occurred in 8.4% and 9.3% of tiragolumab- and control-treated patients, respectively.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04256421 titled 'A Study of Atezolizumab Plus Carboplatin and Etoposide With or Without Tiragolumab in Patients With Untreated Extensive-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints for Clinical Study NCT04256421 included progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) in the full analysis set (FAS), confirmed objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), PFS rates at 6 and 12 months, and OS rates at 12 and 24 months. The study reported hazard ratios (HRs) for PFS and OS in the primary analysis set (PAS). Specifically, the primary analysis of PFS was planned with a target HR of 0.56 at a two-sided significance level of 0.001, while the final analysis of OS was planned with a target HR of 0.70 at a two-sided significance level of 0.049. The study utilized a stratified log-rank test and a stratified Cox proportional hazards model for comparisons. The sample size targeted for the PAS was approximately 400 patients. No specific results for ORR, DOR, or PFS/OS rates at specific time points were mentioned in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Chunk 2: The primary efficacy end points were PFS and OS in all randomly assigned patients without history/presence of brain metastases at baseline (primary analysis set [PAS]). Secondary end points were PFS and OS in all randomly assigned patients, irrespective of brain metastases status at baseline (full analysis set [FAS]); confirmed objective response rate (ORR), defined as the proportion of patients with a complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) on two consecutive occasions ≥4 weeks apart; duration of response (DOR) for patients with confirmed ORR; PFS rate at 6 and 12 months; and OS rate at 12 and 24 months.

Chunk 2: The final analysis of the primary end point, OS in the PAS, was planned for when approximately 288 deaths had been observed in the PAS. This would provide 85% power to detect a target OS HR of 0.70 at a two-sided significance level of 0.049, assuming a median OS of 12.3 months in the placebo plus atezolizumab and CE arm and 17.6 months in the tiragolumab plus atezolizumab and CE arm.
Chunk 2: The stratified log-rank test was used to compare PFS and OS between treatment arms; the HR for PFS and OS was estimated using a stratified Cox proportional hazards model.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04256421 titled 'A Study of Atezolizumab Plus Carboplatin and Etoposide With or Without Tiragolumab in Patients With Untreated Extensive-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the most common adverse events (grade 3+), their incidence rates by arm, and any serious events leading to discontinuation
ground_truth_answer: The most common grade 3/4 adverse events (AEs) in the tiragolumab arm were anemia (36.0%) and neutropenia (25.9%), while in the control arm, they were anemia (37.8%) and neutropenia (30.1%). Serious AEs leading to treatment discontinuation occurred in 8.4% of the tiragolumab arm and 9.3% of the control arm, primarily due to anemia and neutropenia.
source_chunks: Grade 3/4 AEs occurred in 126 patients (52.7%) in the tiragolumab arm and 137 (55.7%) in the control arm, most commonly anemia and neutropenia (Table 3).
AEs leading to withdrawal of any study treatment occurred in 20 patients (8.4%) in the tiragolumab arm and 23 (9.3%) in the control arm.

question: For Clinical Study NCT04256421 titled 'A Study of Atezolizumab Plus Carboplatin and Etoposide With or Without Tiragolumab in Patients With Untreated Extensive-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer:
The primary analysis for Clinical Study NCT04256421 utilized several statistical methods. The stratified log-rank test was employed to compare progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) between treatment arms. The Cox proportional hazards model was used to estimate hazard ratios. Kaplan-Meier methodology was applied to estimate median PFS and OS, with the Brookmeyer-Crowley method used to construct 95% confidence intervals. 

A hierarchical testing approach was implemented, allocating a two-sided alpha of 0.001 to PFS and 0.049 to OS in the primary analysis set (PAS) to maintain an overall type I error rate of 0.05. The sample size targeted approximately 400 patients in the PAS, assuming a 15% prevalence of brain metastases at baseline, with around 470 patients randomly assigned in the study. 

Additionally, the final analysis of OS in the PAS was planned when approximately 288 deaths had occurred, providing 85% power to detect a target OS hazard ratio of 0.70 at a two-sided significance level of 0.049. This comprehensive approach ensured robust statistical analysis of the trial's endpoints.
source_chunks: Survival analyses were performed in the PAS and FAS, with patients grouped according to assigned treatment. [...] The stratified log-rank test was used to compare PFS and OS between treatment arms; the HR for PFS and OS was estimated using a stratified Cox proportional hazards model. Kaplan-Meier methodology was used to estimate median PFS and OS, and the Brookmeyer-Crowley method was used to construct 95% CIs.
To control the overall type 1 error rate at 0.05 (two-sided), endpoints were tested hierarchically. A two-sided α of .001 and .049 was allocated to PFS and OS in the PAS, respectively.

A sample size of approximately 400 patients was targeted for the PAS, assuming a 15% prevalence of presence or history of brain metastases at baseline. It was estimated that approximately 470 patients would be randomly assigned within the study.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05226598 titled 'Study of Pembrolizumab/Vibostolimab Coformulation (MK-7684A) in Combination With Chemotherapy Versus Pembrolizumab Plus Chemotherapy in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-7684A-007/KEYVIBE-007)': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial include adults with histologically or cytologically confirmed, previously untreated, stage IV squamous or nonsquamous NSCLC without EGFR mutations or ALK/ROS1 gene rearrangements. Participants must have measurable disease per RECIST v1.1, an ECOG PS of 0 or 1, and provide a tumor tissue sample for PD-L1 assessment. The age range is not mentioned in these contexts.

Background:Pembrolizumab(pembro)pluschemotherapyisastandardfirst-linetreatmentforpatients
withmetastaticNSCLCwithoutEGFRorALKgenomicalterationsregardlessofPD-L1expression.The
combination of pembro and vibostolimab, a humanized monoclonal antibody that blocks the interac-
tion between the T-cell immunoreceptor with immunoglobulin and ITIM domains (TIGIT) and its li-

advanced or metastatic NSCLC in the phase 1 MK-7684-001 study. The phase 3 KeyVibe-007

Chunk 9: Vibostolimab, a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody, blocks the interaction between TIGIT and its ligands, preventing the immunosuppressive effects of TIGIT. The addition of vibostolimab to the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has shown promising antitumor activity, warranting further exploration of vibostolimab as a potential therapeutic option. The KEYVIBE program consists of nine trials that will evaluate the safety and efficacy of vibostolimab monotherapy and vibostolimab-based combination therapy in advanced solid tumors and hematological malignancies. These studies will also evaluate coformulated immunotherapy addressing issues that occur with the sequential administration of immunotherapy. The KEYVIBE program will provide further insight into the clinical utility of vibostolimab-based therapy across multiple indications and various stages of disease.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05226598 titled 'Study of Pembrolizumab/Vibostolimab Coformulation (MK-7684A) in Combination With Chemotherapy Versus Pembrolizumab Plus Chemotherapy in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-7684A-007/KEYVIBE-007)': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
ground_truth_answer: The KeyVibe-007 trial (NCT05226598) is a randomized, double-blind, phase 3 study with a parallel design. Participants are randomized in a 1:1 ratio to receive either MK-7684A (pembrolizumab and vibostolimab coformulation) plus chemotherapy or pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy. The sample size per arm is not mentioned in the provided contexts.

Chunk 6: Vibostolimab, a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody, blocks the interaction between TIGIT and its ligands, preventing the immunosuppressive effects of TIGIT. The addition of vibostolimab to the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has shown promising antitumor activity, warranting further exploration of vibostolimab as a potential therapeutic option. The KEYVIBE program consists of nine trials that will evaluate the safety and efficacy of vibostolimab monotherapy and vibostolimab-based combination therapy in advanced solid tumors and hematological malignancies. These studies will also evaluate coformulated immunotherapy addressing issues that occur with the sequential administration of immunotherapy. The KEYVIBE program will provide further insight into the clinical utility of vibostolimab-based therapy across multiple indications and various stages of disease.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05226598 titled 'Study of Pembrolizumab/Vibostolimab Coformulation (MK-7684A) in Combination With Chemotherapy Versus Pembrolizumab Plus Chemotherapy in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-7684A-007/KEYVIBE-007)': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the study include overall response rate (ORR) and duration of response per RECIST v1.1 by blinded independent central review (BICR). Additionally, changes from baseline and time to true deterioration in patient-reported outcomes (PROs) were evaluated using validated instruments such as the EORTC Quality of Life Questionnaire Lung Cancer 13 and NSCLC Symptom Assessment Questionnaire. Safety was also assessed, with adverse events graded according to the National Cancer Institute Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events v5.0.
source_chunks: Secondary endpoints are ORR and duration of response per RECIST v1.1 by BICR, change from baseline and time to true deterioration in patient-reported outcomes (PROs), and safety.
PROs are assessed using validated instruments including the EORTC Quality of Life Questionnaire Lung Cancer 13 and NSCLC Symptom Assessment Questionnaire.
AEs are graded according to National Cancer Institute Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events v5.0.

Chunk 5: Vibostolimab, a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody, blocks the interaction between TIGIT and its ligands, preventing the immunosuppressive effects of TIGIT. The addition of vibostolimab to the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has shown promising antitumor activity, warranting further exploration of vibostolimab as a potential therapeutic option. The KEYVIBE program consists of nine trials that will evaluate the safety and efficacy of vibostolimab monotherapy and vibostolimab-based combination therapy in advanced solid tumors and hematological malignancies. These studies will also evaluate coformulated immunotherapy addressing issues that occur with the sequential administration of immunotherapy. The KEYVIBE program will provide further insight into the clinical utility of vibostolimab-based therapy across multiple indications and various stages of disease.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03447769 titled 'Brief Title: Study of Efficacy and Safety of Canakinumab as Adjuvant Therapy in Adult Subjects With Stages AJCC/UICC v. 8 II-IIIA and IIIB (T>5cm N2) Completely Resected Non-small Cell Lung Cancer Acronym: CANOPY-A': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in the CANOPY-A clinical trial included adults with completely resected non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) at stages II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-positive) according to the AJCC/UICC version 8 staging. Patients must have completed at least two cycles of cisplatin-based chemotherapy within 60 days of surgery, except for those with stage IIA disease without nodal involvement, unless recommended by their physician. Eligibility required adequate organ function and an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 0 or 1. No specific biomarkers were mandated for inclusion, although analyses of biomarkers such as EGFR, PD-L1, and CRP were conducted. The exact age range for participants was not specified in the provided contexts.
source_chunks: Patient Population Adult patients with completely resected, AJCC/UICC version 8 stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-positive disease) NSCLC were recruited. Patients had received and recovered from at least two cycles of cisplatin-based therapy within 60 days of surgery (except for patients with stage IIA disease with no nodal involvement, unless recommended by the treating physician) and radiation therapy if indicated per local practice. Eligible patients had adequate organ function and an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of 0 or 1. Patients were eligible regardless of driver mutation status.

Biomarker analyses were conducted using tumor tissue and blood samples. Collection of archival, resected tumor samples during screening was mandatory. Additional tumor samples (core, excisional, and incisional biopsies) were collected at the time of disease recurrence. Correlative analyses reported focus on the baseline tissue collection time point only, because of availability from most patients, and depended on testing priority, tissue quality, and local regulations. Collection of blood samples for circulating biomarker analyses (eg, IL-6 and CRP) was mandatory at baseline, on treatment, at post-treatment follow-up, and at disease progression.

Chunk 3: CANOPY-A (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03447769) is a phase III, randomized, double-blind, multicenter study of canakinumab versus placebo for adult patients with stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-positives II-IIIB; American Joint Committee on Cancer/Union for International Cancer Control version 8), completely resected NSCLC who had received adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy. The primary end point was disease-free survival (DFS) and the key secondary end point was overall survival (OS).

Chunk 4: evaluating the efficacy and safety of canakinumab versus placebo as adjuvant therapy in adult
patients with early-stage, resected NSCLC.
Patients enrolled between April 2018 and December 2021 and were randomly assigned 1:1
(permuted block random assignment) to receive subcutaneous canakinumab 200 mg or placebo
once every 3 weeks for 18 cycles (Figs 1 and 2). Patients were stratified by stage (IIA v IIB v IIIA v
IIIB with T >5 cm, N2-positive disease), as defined according to the American Joint Committee on
Cancer/Union for International Cancer Control (AJCC/UICC) version 8 stage; histology (squamous v
nonsquamous); and geographical region (western Europe and North America v eastern Asia v rest
of the world).

Chunk 5: Patient Characteristics and Disposition
In total, 1,382 patients were randomly assigned to receive canakinumab (n = 693) or placebo (n =
689). Median time from random assignment to data cutoff (March 17, 2022) was 18.9 months
(range, 2.8-47.2). Baseline patient and disease characteristics were well balanced between arms
(Table 1).

multicenter study of canakinumab versus placebo for adult patients with stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T
>5 cm, N2-positives II-IIIB; American Joint Committee on Cancer/Union for International
Cancer Control version 8), completely resected NSCLC who had received adjuvant cisplatin-
based chemotherapy. The primary end point was disease-free survival (DFS) and the key

with data missing for EGFR mutation status and PD-L1, CD8, and CRP levels have been removed. bThe baseline

In light of the COVID-19 pandemic and its potential impacts on treatment effects, a supplementary
analysis censored patients who died or discontinued treatment due to COVID-19, or who started
medication for suspected/confirmed COVID-19. On the basis of this analysis, there was no
treatment effect (HR, 0.96; 95% CI, 0.79 to 1.16).
The key secondary end point, OS, was not formally tested as DFS was not statistically significant
(OS HR, 0.72; 95% CI, 0.48 to 1.08; Fig 4A). Only 100 events were reported: 44 (6.3%) in the
canakinumab arm and 56 (8.1%) in the placebo arm. LCSS (defined as the time from the date of
random assignment to the date of death due to lung cancer) was similar between arms (HR, 0.90;
95% CI, 0.53 to 1.52; Fig 4B). Overall, 60 LCSS events were observed, including 30 events in the
canakinumab arm and 30 events in the placebo arm. Fourteen patients on canakinumab and 26
patients on placebo died due to other reasons and were censored for the LCSS analysis.

Chunk 3: significant. Other secondary end points included DFS and OS by biomarker subgroups, lung
cancer–specific survival (LCSS), safety, pharmacokinetics, and immunogenicity. Details of LCSS
methods and a supplementary analysis to estimate the treatment effects on the basis of DFS had
the COVID-19 pandemic not occurred are provided in Appendix 1 (online only).
Patient Population
Adult patients with completely resected, AJCC/UICC version 8 stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-
positive disease) NSCLC were recruited. Patients had received and recovered from at least two
cycles of cisplatin-based therapy within 60 days of surgery (except for patients with stage IIA
disease with no nodal involvement, unless recommended by the treating physician) and radiation
therapy if indicated per local practice. Eligible patients had adequate organ function and an
Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of 0 or 1. Patients were eligible
regardless of driver mutation status. Patients with unresectable or metastatic disease, malignant
disease other than NSCLC, or lung interstitial disease grade ≥2, and those who had received
neoadjuvant therapy, were ineligible.
Assessments
Imaging (computed tomography [CT] and/or magnetic resonance imaging) was performed at
screening/baseline, once every 12 weeks during year 1, once every 26 weeks during years 2 and 3,
and annually during years 4 and 5. First disease recurrence was detected by CT scan and a biopsy
was performed in cases of nonconclusive radiologic evidence. Patients who discontinued treatment
were followed up once every 12 weeks for survival until the final OS analysis or until death, loss to
follow-up, or consent withdrawal for survival follow-up.
Safety was monitored. All adverse events (AEs) were reported for at least 130 days after the last
administration of canakinumab and graded according to the Common Terminology Criteria for AEs
version 5.
Biomarker analyses were conducted using tumor tissue and blood samples. Collection of archival,
resected tumor samples during screening was mandatory. Additional tumor samples (core,
excisional, and incisional biopsies) were collected at the time of disease recurrence. Correlative
analyses reported focus on the baseline tissue collection time point only, because of availability
from most patients, and depended on testing priority, tissue quality, and local regulations.
Collection of blood samples for circulating biomarker analyses (eg, IL-6 and CRP) was mandatory
at baseline, on treatment, at post-treatment follow-up, and at disease progression. Detailed
biomarker assessments are described in Appendix 1.
Statistical Analysis
The full analysis set comprised all patients assigned to study treatment by random assignment.
DFS of canakinumab versus placebo arms was analyzed using a stratified log-rank test at an overall
one-sided 2.5% significance level. This study had a two-look (Lan-DeMets) group sequential design
with an O'Brien-Fleming type α-spending function. A user-defined γ function (with γ = –3) was

(OS HR, 0.72; 95% CI, 0.48 to 1.08; Fig 4A). Only 100 events were reported: 44 (6.3%) in the

Chunk 6: Additional CANOPY-A study limitations include the long time
from diagnosis to random assignment, based, in part, on the need for chemotherapy in most
patients and radiation therapy in some; short time from random assignment to data cutoff; and
lack of pathologic confirmation of recurrent disease.
Although not formally tested, OS was numerically greater in the canakinumab arm compared with
the placebo arm. Only 100 deaths were reported, and the separation of the Kaplan-Meier curves
occurred after completion of therapy. Furthermore, LCSS rates were similar between the two
treatment arms, with relatively more patient deaths, not from lung cancer, in the placebo arm,
including cardiovascular disease–related fatalities.
Different factors may underpin the disparate results from CANTOS and CANOPY-A, such as the
differences in the populations recruited: patients had cardiovascular disease and myocardial
infarction versus patients with NSCLC, respectively. CRP level was higher in patients diagnosed with
lung cancer in CANTOS than in patients in CANOPY-A (6.0 mg/L v 1.8 mg/L, respectively).16
Nevertheless, the effect of canakinumab on CRP level in CANOPY-A was similar to that observed in
the CANTOS trial, with a significant CRP reduction of up to 41%,16 suggesting that canakinumab

Chunk 7: CANOPY-A (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03447769) is a phase III, randomized, double-blind, multicenter study of canakinumab versus placebo for adult patients with stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-positives II-IIIB; American Joint Committee on Cancer/Union for International Cancer Control version 8), completely resected NSCLC who had received adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy. The primary end point was disease-free survival (DFS) and the key secondary end point was overall survival (OS).

Chunk 8: In total, 1,382 patients were randomized to 200 mg canakinumab (n = 693) or placebo (n =
689) once every 3 weeks for 18 cycles. Grade ≥3 adverse events (AEs) were reported in 20.8%
and 19.6% of patients receiving canakinumab and placebo, respectively; AEs led to
discontinuation in 4.3% and 4.1% of patients in these groups, respectively. This study did not

ground_truth_answer: The most common grade 3+ adverse events (AEs) in the canakinumab and placebo arms were not specifically mentioned in the provided context. source_chunks: Grade ≥3 adverse events (AEs) were reported in 20.8% and 19.6% of patients receiving canakinumab and placebo, respectively; AEs led to discontinuation in 4.3% and 4.1% of patients in these groups, respectively.

Neutropenia incidence was higher in the canakinumab arm compared with the placebo arm (grade 3/4: 1.3% and 0.3%, respectively; no grade 5), as was decreased neutrophil count (grade 3/4: 1.7% and 0.3%, respectively; no grade 5; Appendix Table A2).

AEs leading to dose interruption occurred in 13.4% and 11.0% of patients in the canakinumab and placebo arms, respectively; 5.9% and 3.3%, respectively, were grade 3/4, and no grade 5 AEs leading to dose interruption were reported (Table 2).

ground_truth_answer: The primary analysis for Clinical Study NCT03447769 utilized the Kaplan-Meier method to estimate disease-free survival (DFS) distribution. Chunk 4: CANOPY-A (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03447769) is a phase III, randomized, double-blind, multicenter study of canakinumab versus placebo for adult patients with stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-positives II-IIIB; American Joint Committee on Cancer/Union for International Cancer Control version 8), completely resected NSCLC who had received adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy. The primary end point was disease-free survival (DFS) and the key secondary end point was overall survival (OS).

Chunk 5: CANOPY-A (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03447769) is a phase III, randomized, double-blind,
multicenter study of canakinumab versus placebo for adult patients with stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T
>5 cm, N2-positives II-IIIB; American Joint Committee on Cancer/Union for International
Cancer Control version 8), completely resected NSCLC who had received adjuvant cisplatin-
based chemotherapy. The primary end point was disease-free survival (DFS) and the key
secondary end point was overall survival (OS).

Chunk 6: evaluating the efficacy and safety of canakinumab versus placebo as adjuvant therapy in adult
patients with early-stage, resected NSCLC.
Patients enrolled between April 2018 and December 2021 and were randomly assigned 1:1
(permuted block random assignment) to receive subcutaneous canakinumab 200 mg or placebo
once every 3 weeks for 18 cycles (Figs 1 and 2). Patients were stratified by stage (IIA v IIB v IIIA v
IIIB with T >5 cm, N2-positive disease), as defined according to the American Joint Committee on
Cancer/Union for International Cancer Control (AJCC/UICC) version 8 stage; histology (squamous v
nonsquamous); and geographical region (western Europe and North America v eastern Asia v rest
of the world).

Chunk 7: CANOPY-A did not show a DFS benefit of adding canakinumab after surgery and adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy in patients with resected, stage II-III NSCLC. No new safety signals were identified with canakinumab.

Chunk 8: In an exploratory analysis of the CANTOS trial, reductions in lung cancer incidence and mortality
were seen, which led to the hypothesis that canakinumab might improve clinical outcomes for
patients with NSCLC. To test this, a suite of CANOPY clinical studies was opened to explore
canakinumab treatment for NSCLC in various settings. Although the CANOPY-1 and CANOPY-2
(unpublished data) studies did not show improvements in progression-free survival or OS in either
the advanced or metastatic settings in either trial, patient-reported outcomes from CANOPY-1
suggested beneficial effects of canakinumab on symptoms.18-20 By contrast, the CANOPY-A study
aimed to explore the efficacy of canakinumab in the early-stage disease setting in patients without
evidence of disease after complete resection (followed by adjuvant chemotherapy and/or
radiotherapy when applicable), a more relevant setting to test the hypothesis on the basis of
CANTOS data. Of note, the CANOPY-N study investigated canakinumab as a single agent and in
combination with a checkpoint inhibitor in the neoadjuvant setting in patients who were
treatment-naïve. Decreases in inflammation markers and immunosuppressive features in the tumor
microenvironment were observed with IL-1β inhibition, but there was minimal single-agent
activity.21
In CANOPY-A, the primary end point of DFS was not met, and subgroup analyses of DFS, including
by smoking history, were explored. In the CANTOS study, patients who developed lung cancer
generally had a history of smoking, and the results suggested that canakinumab had a slightly
stronger effect in current versus former smokers. This observation seemed consistent with the
anticipated effects of canakinumab, as patients with a history of smoking would be expected to be
more likely to develop inflammatory disease and, thus, be more likely to derive benefit from
canakinumab treatment.22 Yet, in CANOPY-A, there was no benefit of canakinumab in subgroups of
patients defined by smoking status. Patients were enrolled in CANOPY-A from April 11, 2018, to
December 23, 2021, during which time new data were released for checkpoint inhibitors in the
adjuvant setting. We cannot exclude the possibility that the change in treatment pattern after the
approval of immunotherapy for patients who are PD-L1–positive may have resulted in a larger PD-
L1–low/–negative population in CANOPY-A; however, there was no clear trend across PD-L1
subgroups for canakinumab benefit. Additional CANOPY-A study limitations include the long time
from diagnosis to random assignment, based, in part, on the need for chemotherapy in most
patients and radiation therapy in some; short time from random assignment to data cutoff; and
lack of pathologic confirmation of recurrent disease.
Although not formally tested, OS was numerically greater in the canakinumab arm compared with
the placebo arm. Only 100 deaths were reported, and the separation of the Kaplan-Meier curves
occurred after completion of therapy. Furthermore, LCSS rates were similar between the two
treatment arms, with relatively more patient deaths, not from lung cancer, in the placebo arm,
including cardiovascular disease–related fatalities.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03976375 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Lenvatinib (E7080/MK-7902) vs. Docetaxel in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) and Progressive Disease (PD) After Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy (MK-7902-008/E7080-G000-316/LEAP-008)': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: Participants were required to be at least 18 years old with histologically or cytologically confirmed metastatic squamous or non-squamous NSCLC (stage IV per AJCC 8th edition). They must have had measurable disease per RECIST 1.1, an ECOG performance status of 0 or 1, and a life expectancy of at least 3 months. They needed to have progressive disease after platinum-doublet chemotherapy and prior anti–PD-1 or anti–PD-L1 therapy. Available archival tumor tissue for PD-L1 evaluation was required.
source_chunks: Participants also had measurable disease per RECIST version 1.1, an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 0 or 1, a life expectancy of at least 3 months, and available archival (i.e., obtained any time from the initial diagnosis to before receiving immunotherapy) tumor tissue sample for PD-L1 evaluation.

Participants were randomized to receive oral lenvatinib 20 mg once daily plus intravenous pembrolizumab 200 mg every 3 weeks for 35 cycles or less (lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab arm), intravenous docetaxel 75 mg/m2 every 3 weeks (docetaxel arm), or oral lenvatinib 24 mg monotherapy once daily.

Eligible participants (aged (cid:2)18 years) had histologically or cytologically confirmed metastatic squamous or non-squamous NSCLC (stage IV per American Joint Committee on Cancer staging manual, eighth edition) for which EGFR-, ALK-, or ROS1-directed therapy was not indicated, and investigator-confirmed PD (per Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors [RECIST] version 1.1) after treatment with one prior anti–PD-1 or anti–PD-L1 monoclonal antibody.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03976375 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Lenvatinib (E7080/MK-7902) vs. Docetaxel in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) and Progressive Disease (PD) After Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy (MK-7902-008/E7080-G000-316/LEAP-008)': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
ground_truth_answer:
The exclusion criteria for the study included:  
1. Clinically significant hemoptysis or tumor bleeding 2 weeks or less before study treatment.  
2. Intratumoral cavitation, encasement, or invasion of a major blood vessel.  
3. Significant cardiovascular impairment 12 months or less before study treatment.  
4. A gastrointestinal condition or procedure that might affect drug absorption.  
5. A preexisting grade 3 or higher fistula.  
6. Immunodeficiency or receipt of long-term systemic steroids or immunosuppressive therapy 7 days or less before study treatment.  
7. Any other known malignancy, active central nervous system metastasis, carcinomatous meningitis, or active autoimmune disease requiring systemic treatment within 2 years or less.  
8. An active infection requiring systemic therapy, or a history of human immunodeficiency virus or hepatitis B or C infection.  
9. A history of noninfectious pneumonitis requiring systemic steroids or current pneumonitis or interstitial lung disease.  
10. Any other condition that might affect the study results.
source_chunks: Participants were excluded if they had any of the following: (1) clinically significant hemoptysis or tumor bleeding 2 weeks or less before study treatment; (2) intratumoral cavitation, encasement, or invasion of a major blood vessel; (3) significant cardiovascular impairment 12 months or less before study treatment; (4) a gastrointestinal condition or procedure that might affect drug absorption; (5) a preexisting grade 3 or higher fistula; (6) immunodeficiency or received long-term systemic steroids or immunosuppressive therapy 7 days or less before study treatment; (7) any other known malignancy, active central nervous system metastasis, carcinomatous meningeal disease, or active autoimmune disease requiring systemic treatment within 2 years or less; (8) an active infection requiring systemic therapy, or a history of human immunodeficiency virus or hepatitis B or C infection; (9) a history of noninfectious pneumonitis requiring systemic steroids or current pneumonitis or interstitial lung disease; (10) any other condition that might affect the study results.

Chunk 6: LEAP-008 (NCT03976375) was an open-label, randomized, phase 3 study of lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab versus docetaxel for metastatic NSCLC that progressed on anti‒programmed cell death protein 1 or anti‒programmed cell death ligand 1 therapy and platinum-containing chemotherapy.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03976375 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Lenvatinib (E7080/MK-7902) vs. Docetaxel in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) and Progressive Disease (PD) After Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy (MK-7902-008/E7080-G000-316/LEAP-008)': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
ground_truth_answer: The trial was a randomized, double-blind, parallel study. The sample size per arm was 180 participants in the lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab arm, 180 in the docetaxel arm, and 45 in the lenvatinib monotherapy arm.
source_chunks: Participants were randomized 4:4:1 by means of an interactive response technology system to receive oral lenvatinib 20 mg once daily plus intravenous pembrolizumab 200 mg every 3 weeks for 35 cycles or less (lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab arm), intravenous docetaxel 75 mg/m2 every 3 weeks (docetaxel arm), or oral lenvatinib 24 mg monotherapy once daily.
The planned sample size was 405 participants, including 45 participants in the lenvatinib arm.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03976375 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Lenvatinib (E7080/MK-7902) vs. Docetaxel in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) and Progressive Disease (PD) After Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy (MK-7902-008/E7080-G000-316/LEAP-008)': What was the primary endpoint of this study, how was it measured (e.g., PFS at 12 months, response rate), and what was the predefined success threshold
ground_truth_answer: The dual primary endpoints of the study were Overall Survival (OS) and Progression-Free Survival (PFS). OS was measured as the time from randomization to death from any cause, and PFS was measured as the time from randomization to the first documented progression of disease (PD) or death from any cause, both assessed per RECIST version 1.1 by blinded independent central review (BICR). The predefined success thresholds were based on hazard ratios (HR) of 0.6 for PFS and 0.66 for OS, with corresponding one-sided alpha levels of 0.29% for PFS and 1.9% for OS.
source_chunks: The dual primary end points were OS (time from randomization to death from any cause) and PFS (time from randomization to first documented PD or death from any cause) per RECIST version 1.1 by BICR.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03976375 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Lenvatinib (E7080/MK-7902) vs. Docetaxel in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) and Progressive Disease (PD) After Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy (MK-7902-008/E7080-G000-316/LEAP-008)': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
ground_truth_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the clinical trial included overall response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), adverse events (AEs), discontinuations due to AEs, mean changes in health-related quality of life (HRQoL) scores such as global health status (GHS), dyspnea, physical functioning, cough, and chest pain. Additionally, time to true deterioration (TTD) in GHS, dyspnea, physical functioning, cough, chest pain, and a composite symptom endpoint were evaluated. Exploratory endpoints, such as subgroup analyses of progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) based on the best response to prior anti-PD-1 or anti-PD-L1 therapy, were not mentioned in these contexts.
source_chunks: Secondary end points included ORR and duration of response (DOR) per RECIST version 1.1 by BICR; adverse events (AEs); discontinuations because of AEs; mean changes from baseline in health-related quality of life scores (HRQoL), including global health status (GHS)/QoL, dyspnea, and physical functioning scores (assessed using the European Organization for Research and Treatment of Cancer [EORTC] Quality-of-Life Questionnaire–Core 30 [QLQ-C30]), and cough and chest pain scores (assessed using the EORTC Quality-of-Life Questionnaire–13-item Lung Cancer Module [QLQ-LC13]); and time to true deterioration (TTD) in GHS/QoL, dyspnea, physical functioning, cough, chest pain, and the composite symptom end point of dyspnea, cough, and chest pain.

Subgroup analyses of PFS per RECIST version 1.1 by BICR and OS were conducted in both prespecified and exploratory post hoc groups. The exploratory post hoc group assessed participants on the basis of the best response to immediate previous anti–PD-1 or anti–PD-L1 therapy.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03976375 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Lenvatinib (E7080/MK-7902) vs. Docetaxel in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) and Progressive Disease (PD) After Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy (MK-7902-008/E7080-G000-316/LEAP-008)': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint results for the study NCT03976375 showed that the median PFS was 5.6 months (95% CI: 4.2–6.5) for the lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab arm and 4.2 months (95% CI: 3.2–5.2) for the docetaxel arm, with a hazard ratio (HR) of 0.89 (95% CI: 0.70–1.12) and a p-value of 0.164. The primary objective of demonstrating superiority in PFS was not met as the p-value exceeded the superiority threshold. For OS, the HR was 0.98 (95% CI: 0.78–1.23) with a p-value of 0.434, indicating that the primary objective was also not met for OS.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03976375 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Lenvatinib (E7080/MK-7902) vs. Docetaxel in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) and Progressive Disease (PD) After Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy (MK-7902-008/E7080-G000-316/LEAP-008)': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings included assessments of health-related quality of life (HRQoL) using EORTC QLQ-C30 and QLQ-LC13 questionnaires, with completion and compliance rates reported. Safety was evaluated, showing common treatment-related adverse events (AEs) such as hypertension, hypothyroidism, diarrhea, asthenia, decreased appetite, and proteinuria. Significant adjustments included the use of the Lan-DeMets O'Brien-Fleming method for multiple testing, with an alpha allocation of 0.019 among interim and final analyses for overall survival (OS). Specific hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences for secondary endpoints were not mentioned in these contexts.
source_chunks: HRQoL was assessed in the patient-reported outcomes (PRO) population, including all randomized participants who had at least one PRO assessment and received one or more doses of the study drug.
Completion and compliance rates for both the EORTC QLQ-C30 and QLQ-LC13 questionnaires were high.

AEs were monitored throughout the study until 30 days (90 days for serious AEs) after the last dose of the study drug, with severity graded according to National Cancer Institute Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events version 4.0.
The Lan-DeMets O'Brien-Fleming method was used to allocate an alpha of 0.019 among the interim and final analyses for OS.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03976375 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Lenvatinib (E7080/MK-7902) vs. Docetaxel in Participants With Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) and Progressive Disease (PD) After Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy (MK-7902-008/E7080-G000-316/LEAP-008)': What statistical methods were used for the primary analysis (e.g., Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, ITT vs. per-protocol), including multiplicity corrections
ground_truth_answer: The primary analyses for the clinical trial NCT03976375 utilized several statistical methods. For overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS), between-group differences were estimated using a stratified log-rank test. Hazard ratios (HRs) were calculated with a stratified Cox regression model employing the Efron handling method. The Kaplan-Meier method was used to estimate event rates over time. Objective response rates (ORR) were assessed using the stratified Miettinen and Nurminen method, while duration of response (DOR) was analyzed using summary statistics and the Kaplan-Meier method. The intention-to-treat (ITT) population was the primary analysis set, including all randomized participants. Multiplicity corrections were applied using the Lan-DeMets O’Brien-Fleming group sequential method and the Maurer-Bretz graphical method with preallocated alpha levels for different endpoints.

question: For Clinical Study NCT05740566 titled 'Study Comparing Tarlatamab With Standard of Care Chemotherapy in Relapsed Small Cell Lung Cancer': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
ground_truth_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in the clinical trial NCT05740566 included adults with histologically or cytologically confirmed relapsed small cell lung cancer (SCLC) who had progressed following one platinum-based regimen. The exact age range was not specified, but participants were required to have measurable lesions as defined per RECIST 1.1 within a 21-day screening period and adequate organ function. Required biomarkers were not mentioned in the provided contexts. Key exclusions included untreated or symptomatic central nervous system metastases, a history of immune checkpoint inhibitor use resulting in severe immune-mediated adverse events, and a previous history of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).
source_chunks: Key eligibility criteria include adultswith histologically or cytologically confirmed relapsed SCLC who progressed following 1 platinum-based regimen. Patients must have measurable lesions as defined per RECIST 1.1 within the 21-day screeningperiodandmusthaveadequateorganfunction.Exclusionsincludeuntreatedorsymptomatic central nervous system metastases, history of immune checkpoint inhibitor use resulting in severe immune-mediatedadverseevent,andprevioushistoryofNSCLC.
Patients were randomly assigned to receive tarlatamab or chemotherapy (topotecan, lurbinectedin, or amrubicin).

Chunk 4: Treatment with tarlatamab led to longer overall survival than chemotherapy among patients with small-cell lung cancer whose disease had progressed during or after platinum-based chemotherapy. (Funded by Amgen; DeLLphi-304 ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT05740566.).

Chunk 5: We conducted a multinational, phase 3, open-label trial to compare tarlatamab with chemotherapy as second-line treatment in patients with small-cell lung cancer whose disease had progressed during or after platinum-based chemotherapy. Patients were randomly assigned to receive tarlatamab or chemotherapy (topotecan, lurbinectedin, or amrubicin). The primary end point was overall survival. Key secondary end points were investigator-assessed progression-free survival and patient-reported outcomes. Results of the prespecified interim analysis (data-cutoff date, January 29, 2025) are reported.

Chunk 6: Tarlatamab, a bispecific delta-like ligand 3-directed T-cell engager immunotherapy, received accelerated approval for the treatment of patients with previously treated small-cell lung cancer. Whether tarlatamab is more effective than chemotherapy in the treatment of patients whose small-cell lung cancer has progressed during or after initial platinum-based chemotherapy is not known.

Chunk 7: A total of 509 patients were randomly assigned to receive tarlatamab (254 patients) or chemotherapy (255 patients). Treatment with tarlatamab resulted in significantly longer overall survival than chemotherapy (median, 13.6 months [95% confidence interval {CI}, 11.1 to not reached] vs. 8.3 months [95% CI, 7.0 to 10.2]; stratified hazard ratio for death, 0.60; 95% CI, 0.47 to 0.77; P<0.001). Tarlatamab treatment also had a significant benefit with respect to progression-free survival and cancer-related dyspnea and cough as compared with chemotherapy. The incidence of adverse events of grade 3 or higher was lower with tarlatamab than with chemotherapy (54% vs. 80%), as was the incidence of adverse events resulting in treatment discontinuation (5% vs. 12%).

question: For Clinical Study NCT05740566 titled 'Study Comparing Tarlatamab With Standard of Care Chemotherapy in Relapsed Small Cell Lung Cancer': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
ground_truth_answer: The trial was a randomized, open-label, phase 3 study comparing tarlatamab with standard of care chemotherapy in patients with relapsed small-cell lung cancer. The trial design was parallel, with patients randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to receive either tarlatamab or chemotherapy. The sample size per arm was approximately 350 patients for each group.
source_chunks: Chunk 1: We conducted a multinational, phase 3, open-label trial to compare tarlatamab with chemotherapy as second-line treatment in patients with small-cell lung cancer whose disease had progressed during or after platinum-based chemotherapy. Chunk 5: Methods: NCT05740566 is a randomized, open-label, phase 3 study of tarlatamab compared with standard of care (SOC) in ~700 patients with relapsed SCLC after platinum-based first-line chemotherapy. Patients will be randomized 1:1 to receive tarlatamab or SOC therapy (lurbinecte-din or topotecan in US, Canada, Australia, Singapore, Korea; amrubicin in Japan; topotecan in all countries except Japan), stratified by prior anti-programmed cell death 1 (PD-1) or anti-programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1) exposure, chemotherapy-free interval, presence of brain metastases, and SOC (topotecan/amrubicin vs lurbinectedin).

Chunk 7: A total of 509 patients were randomly assigned to receive tarlatamab (254 patients) or chemotherapy (255 patients). Treatment with tarlatamab resulted in significantly longer overall survival than chemotherapy (median, 13.6 months [95% confidence interval {CI}, 11.1 to not reached] vs. 8.3 months [95% CI, 7.0 to 10.2]; stratified hazard ratio for death, 0.60; 95% CI, 0.47 to 0.77; P<0.001). Tarlatamab treatment also had a significant benefit with respect to progression-free survival and cancer-related dyspnea and cough as compared with chemotherapy. The incidence of adverse events of grade 3 or higher was lower with tarlatamab than with chemotherapy (54% vs. 80%), as was the incidence of adverse events resulting in treatment discontinuation (5% vs. 12%).

question: For Clinical Study NCT05740566 titled 'Study Comparing Tarlatamab With Standard of Care Chemotherapy in Relapsed Small Cell Lung Cancer': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings included significant improvements in both progression-free survival (PFS) and patient-reported outcomes (PROs) with tarlatamab compared to chemotherapy. Chunk 9: Tarlatamab, a bispecific delta-like ligand 3-directed T-cell engager immunotherapy, received accelerated approval for the treatment of patients with previously treated small-cell lung cancer. Whether tarlatamab is more effective than chemotherapy in the treatment of patients whose small-cell lung cancer has progressed during or after initial platinum-based chemotherapy is not known.

Chunk 10: A total of 509 patients were randomly assigned to receive tarlatamab (254 patients) or chemotherapy (255 patients). Treatment with tarlatamab resulted in significantly longer overall survival than chemotherapy (median, 13.6 months [95% confidence interval {CI}, 11.1 to not reached] vs. 8.3 months [95% CI, 7.0 to 10.2]; stratified hazard ratio for death, 0.60; 95% CI, 0.47 to 0.77; P<0.001). Tarlatamab treatment also had a significant benefit with respect to progression-free survival and cancer-related dyspnea and cough as compared with chemotherapy. The incidence of adverse events of grade 3 or higher was lower with tarlatamab than with chemotherapy (54% vs. 80%), as was the incidence of adverse events resulting in treatment discontinuation (5% vs. 12%).

ground_truth_answer: The LAURA trial (NCT03521154) is a Phase 3, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel study. Patients were randomized in a 2:1 ratio, with 143 receiving osimertinib and 73 receiving placebo. The trial was not crossover designed.

stage III EGFR-mutated NSCLC after definitive chemoradiotherapy given the statistically and clinically significant improve-
ment in PFS compared to placebo. Furthermore, osimertinib provides a protective effect against distant metastases and CNS
progression in patients with unresectable stage III EGFR-mutated NSCLC following CRT. Together with the primary PFS
benefit, reduced risk of CNS progression and prolongation of TTDM, the LAURA study supports the use of osimertinib as the
new standard of care in patients with unresectable stage III EGFR-mutated NSCLC following CRT. This exciting finding also
advocates for the importance of evaluating the appropriate targeted therapy as adjuvant treatment in unresectable NSCLC with
other driver alterations such as ALK, ROS1, and RET mutations through future clinical trials.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03521154 titled 'A Global Study to Assess the Effects of Osimertinib Following Chemoradiation in Patients With Stage III Unresectable Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (LAURA)': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
ground_truth_answer: The primary endpoint of the LAURA trial (NCT03521154) was progression-free survival (PFS). The results showed that osimertinib significantly improved PFS compared to placebo. The median PFS for the osimertinib group was 39.1 months (95% CI: 31.5 to not calculable), while the placebo group had a median PFS of 5.6 months (95% CI: 3.0–8.9). The hazard ratio for disease progression or death was 0.16 (95% CI: 0.10–0.24), with a p-value of <0.001, indicating a statistically significant reduction in the risk of disease progression or death. The primary objective was met, demonstrating the efficacy of osimertinib in improving PFS for patients with unresectable stage III EGFR-mutated NSCLC following chemoradiation.

Chunk 5: The LAURA study demonstrated a significant benefit to PFS with osimertinib treatment (median PFS [mPFS] 39.1
months) after definitive CRT compared to placebo (mPFS 5.6 months) with HR for disease progression or death of 0.16
(95% CI: 0.10–0.24; P<0.001), equivalent to an 84% reduction in the risk of disease progression or death with
osimertinib compared with placebo.
Currently, the standard of treatment for unresectable stage III NSCLC is CRT followed by consolidation therapy with
immune checkpoint inhibitor durvalumab.5,6 Prior to the LAURA trial, durvalumab was the only adjuvant maintenance
therapy that was approved as consolidation therapy in unresectable stage III NSCLC through the PACIFIC trial.
However, durvalumab did not result in PFS or OS improvement in EGFR-mutated or ALK-rearranged NSCLC.18 In
fact, the mPFS with durvalumab was similar to that with placebo (11.2 months [95% CI: 7.3–20.7] vs 10.9 months [95%
CI: 1.9-not evaluable], respectively) in a post hoc subgroup analysis of patients with EGFR-mutated NSCLC.19 The
LAURA trial helps fill this gap in treatment for unresectable stage III, EGFR-mutated NSCLC after CRT given the
efficacy of osimertinib in significantly improving PFS, with mPFS being 33.6 months longer compared to placebo.
Additionally, LAURA has demonstrated efficacy in lowering incidence of distant metastases, including in the CNS. The
osimertinib group had lower incidence of new lesions (22%) than placebo group (68%), including new brain lesions (8% vs 29%,

question: For Clinical Study NCT03521154 titled 'A Global Study to Assess the Effects of Osimertinib Following Chemoradiation in Patients With Stage III Unresectable Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (LAURA)': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
ground_truth_answer: The LAURA study (NCT03521154) investigated osimertinib following chemoradiation in Stage III unresectable NSCLC. Key secondary endpoint findings included significant reductions in the risk of CNS progression or death and distant metastases or death with osimertinib compared to placebo. Specifically, the hazard ratio (HR) for CNS progression-free survival (PFS) was 0.17 (95% CI: 0.09–0.32, P<0.001), and for time to death or distant metastases (TTDM), the HR was 0.21 (95% CI: 0.11–0.38, P<0.001). The objective response rate (ORR) was also significantly higher in the osimertinib group with an odds ratio (OR) of 2.77 (95% CI: 1.54–5.08). Safety analysis showed that while adverse events were common, most were mild to moderate, with radiation pneumonitis being the most frequent. No significant adjustments to the study were noted in the available information.
source_chunks: Chunk 4: receptor-mutated non-small-cell lung cancer: analysis of central nervous system efficacy and distant progression from the phase III LAURA study. Ann Oncol. 2024;25(suppl_2):S794–S801. doi:10.1016/j.annonc.2024.08.1299

Chunk 2: osimertinib over placebo across all subgroups with hazard ratios ranging from 0.16 to 0.48. This included patients of all
WHO performance-status scores.
The incidence of local progression and distant metastases were lower in the osimertinib group (21% and 16%,
respectively) compared to the placebo group (48% and 37%, respectively). Importantly, the osimertinib group had lower
incidence of new lesions (22%) than placebo group (68%), including new brain lesions (8% vs 29%, respectively). In the
secondary analysis of CNS PFS by neuroradiologist BICR, median CNS PFS was not reached with osimertinib compared
to 14.9 months (95% CI: 7.4-NC) with placebo. With osimertinib, there was an 83% reduction in the risk of CNS
progression or death compared to placebo [HR 0.17 (95% CI: 0.09–0.32), P<0.001]. In the secondary analysis of time to
death or distant metastases (TTDM) by BICR, median TTDM for osimertinib was also not reached (95% CI: 39.3-NC)
compared to 13 months (95% CI: 9-NC) with placebo. There was a 79% reduction in the risk of distant metastases or
death with osimertinib versus placebo [HR 0.21 (95% CI: 0.11–0.38), P<0.001].17
At 36 months, the overall survival for the osimertinib group was 84% (95% CI: 75–89) compared to 74% (95% CI:
57–85) for the placebo group (HZ = 0.81; 95% CI: 0.42–1.56; P=0.53). This was not significant at this interim analysis,
although it is important to note the objective response rate (ORR) was higher in the osimertinib group than in the placebo
group (57% [95% CI: 49–66] versus 33% [95% CI: 22–45]; odds ratio (OR) = 2.77 [95% CI: 1.54–5.08]).16
In terms of safety, 140 patients (98%) receiving osimertinib and 64 patients (88%) receiving placebo reported adverse
events. The most common adverse events, regardless of cause, were radiation pneumonitis (48% with osimertinib vs 38%
with placebo), diarrhea (36% vs 14%) and rash (24% vs 14%). Grade 3 or higher adverse events were reported in 50
patients (35%) in the osimertinib group and 9 patients (12%) in placebo group.16
Chunk 3: subgroup analysis from PACIFIC. J Thorac Oncol. 2023;18(5):657–663. doi:10.1016/j.jtho.2023.02.009
Chunk 4: from the PACIFIC trial. J Thorac Oncol. 2016;16:860–867. doi:10.1016/j.jtho.2020.12.015

Chunk 6: Luo et al
respectively). This is significant as the presence of distant metastases in the CNS is a poor prognostic factor that negatively
impacts quality of life, Moreover, studies have shown that patients with unresectable stage III, EGFR-mutated NSCLC after
definite CRT have a higher tendency to develop distant metastases, including in the CNS, compared to patients without EGFR
mutations.18,20,21 Formal statistical testing has yet to be completed for CNS PFS pending whether OS data reach statistical
significance (planned at 60% maturity); however, the magnitude of CNS PFS and TTDM improvements are considered clinically
meaningful. The efficacy of osimertinib in significantly reducing the risk of distant metastases and CNS progression compared to
placebo in patients with unresectable stage III EGFR-mutated NSCLC after CRT further solidifies its efficacy in such patients,
helping address an unmet need against CNS progression and distant metastases in this patient population.
Importantly, the safety and tolerability of osimertinib remained consistent with its established profile. The majority of
reported adverse effects were mild to moderate in terms of severity (grade 1–3) and did not lead to discontinuation. The most
frequently reported grade 3 or higher adverse events in both treatment groups was pneumonia (3% with osimertinib, 4% with
placebo). Other grade 3 or higher adverse events reported included radiation pneumonitis and diarrhea though both were
previously known and expected adverse events either with osimertinib or CRT. Previous reports highlighted possible
association of increased risk of radiation pneumonitis with the use of EGFR-TKIs together with CRT or radiotherapy.7,10 In
the LAURA trial, the most frequently reported adverse event in both osimertinib and placebo arms was radiation pneumonitis
with no significant difference (48% vs 38%, respectively). The overall incidence of grade 3 pneumonitis was low at 2%, and
there were no grade 4 or 5 pneumonitis reported.16 Radiation pneumonitis of any grade was reported in 48% of patients
receiving osimertinib after CRT, which is equivocal or decreased compared to the incidence of radiation pneumonitis with the
use of durvalumab (59%) in EGFR-mutated NSCLC after CRT as seen in the PACIFIC trial, though this cross-trial comparison
should be interpreted with caution.7,16 To note, CRT is a risk factor for the development of pneumonitis and is likely
a contributor to the increased incidence seen in both arms and both trials.22 To summarize, the incidence of radiation
pneumonitis as an adverse event was similar between both osimertinib and placebo arms. Additionally, osimertinib may
lead to a reduced or at least equivocal incidence of radiation pneumonitis compared to durvalumab.
In addition to following up on the final OS, CNS PFS, safety analysis of the LAURA trial, future research evaluating
the efficacy and safety of EGFR-TKIs as either induction treatment before CRT or concomitantly with CRT in EGFR-
mutated unresectable stage III NSCLC would be of interest.
